semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
In physics, momenta 
%$\bold p_1,\ldots,\bold p_n$ 
of $n$ particles satisfy the momentum conservation law.
$\bold p_1+\ldots+\bold p_n=0$.
In~mathematics,  meromorphic forms $\omega$ %\in H^0(C,\omega_C(p_1+\ldots+p_n))$
with simple poles $p_1,\ldots,p_n$ (log forms) on 
a compact Riemann surface
%$(C; p_1,\ldots,p_n)$
%with marked points
%smooth (or stable) pointed  algebraic curve 
% and a log canonical form $\omega\in H^0(C,\omega_C(p_1+\ldots+p_n))$,
satisfy the residue theorem:
\vskip-2pt
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\makebox[0.32\textwidth]{\vbox{$$\bold p_1+\ldots+\bold p_n=0$$\\ \ \\ \ }}
\makebox[0.25\textwidth]{\vbox{\includegraphics[height=0.8in]{mosquito.pdf}}}
\ \ \ \makebox[0.32\textwidth]{\vbox{$$\operatorname{Res}_{p_1}\omega+\ldots+\operatorname{Res}_{p_n}\omega=0$$\\ \ \\ \ }}
%\caption{Residue Theorem}
\end{figure}
\vskip-2pt
\noindent
Let $C$ be a stable  projective algebraic  curve with marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_n$. The exact sequence 
 $0\to H^0(C,\omega_C)\to H^0(C,\omega_C(p_1+\ldots+p_n))\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{\operatorname{Res}}\Bbb C^n \mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{\Sigma}\Bbb C\to0$
 (valid for $n\ge1$)
 shows that we can always view momenta of $n$  one-dimensional particles satisfying the momentum conservation law
as residues of a form $\omega\in H^0(C,\omega_C(p_1+\ldots+p_n))$. For a smooth curve $C$, 
ambiguities in the choice of $\omega$ can be eliminated by fixing its integrals along periods.
For a nodal curve $C$, a local section of $\omega_C(p_1+\ldots+p_n)$ at each node $q_1,\ldots,q_r\in C$ can be identified with a log form on
the normalization~$C^\nu$ which has opposite residues at the points $q_i^-$, $q_i^+$ mapping to the node $q_i$. 
These residues can be viewed as momenta of ``internal'' or "on-shell" particles satisfying a momentum conservation law 
for each of the $s$ irreducible components of $C$, as follows from an exact sequence
$$0\to H^0(C^\nu,\omega_{C^\nu})\to H^0(C,\omega_C(p_1+\ldots+p_n))\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{\operatorname{Res}}\Bbb C^{n+r} \mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{\Sigma_1,\ldots,\Sigma_s}\Bbb C^s\to0.$$
When all components of $C$ are rational, $H^0(C^\nu,\omega_{C^\nu})=0$ and the datum of a log-form on $C$ is equivalent to the datum of external and internal momenta
satisfying momentum conservation laws (one law for each irreducible component of~$C$). Equivalently, 
this is the datum of one ``edge variable'' for each internal (corresponding to a node) and external
(corresponding to a marked point) 
edge of the dual graph of $C$ with a conservation law for each vertex of the dual graph.

entries.lean_statement: theorem afqrgqrb (C : Type*) (p : Fin n → C) (d : ℤ) (d : ℤ) (g : ℕ) (n : ℕ) (h_deg : d + d = 2 * g - 2 + n) (h_smooth : Prop) : ∃ (mu : Measure (Pic ^ (d : ℤ) C)), mu = volume_form_invariant_under_translations (Pic ^ (0 : ℤ) C) ∧ mu (Pic ^ (d : ℤ) C) = 1

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
Imagine a random process that gives 
a vector of log forms  on $(C; p_1,\ldots,p_n)$.
What will be the probability  distribution of 
residues? We will study the following 
 simple random process.
The~first step  is a random tensor product factorization
\begin{equation}\label{afqrgqrb}
\omega_C(p_1+\ldots+p_n)=L\otimes\tilde L,
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
into two line bundles, $L$ of degree $d$ and $\tilde L$ of degree $\tilde d$ 
(and $d+\tilde d=2g-2+n$).
%In the stable case one needs to further specify a vector of degrees on irreducible components of $C$. 
The~choice of $L$ is a choice of a  point in $\operatorname{Pic}^dC$
and, when $C$ is smooth,  every connected component of the Picard group 
has a uniform probability measure,  the volume form invariant under translations
by the subgroup $\operatorname{Pic}^0C$ of topologically trivial line bundles.
%\footnote{
If~$C$ is not smooth  then
$\operatorname{Pic}^0C$ contains non-compact factors~$\Bbb C^*$ and probabilistic interpretation is less straightforward.
In fact the case most related to physics literature is when all irreducible components of $C$ are rational curves
and  $\operatorname{Pic}^0C\simeq(\Bbb C^*)^g$.
Non-compactness will not be an issue for us.
%}
semantic_equivalence_artifact.normalized_natural_language_theorem: Quantifiers: a; every; when Assumptions: C is smooth; d + d̃ = 2g - 2 + n; all irreducible components of C are rational curves Conclusion: The probability distribution of residues for a random process giving a vector of log forms on (C; p_1, ..., p_n) is determined by the random tensor product factorization ω_C(p_1 + ... + p_n) = L ⊗ L̃, where L is chosen uniformly from Pic^d C with a volume form invariant under translations by Pic^0 C.

all irreducible components of C are rational curves
semantic_equivalence_artifact.extracted_conclusion: The probability distribution of residues for a random process giving a vector of log forms on (C; p_1, ..., p_n) is determined by the random tensor product factorization ω_C(p_1 + ... + p_n) = L ⊗ L̃, where L is chosen uniformly from Pic^d C with a volume form invariant under translations by Pic^0 C.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{Review} Imagine a random process that gives a vector of log forms on $(C; p_1,\ldots,p_n)$. What will be the probability distribution of residues? We will study the following simple random process. The~first step is a random tensor product factorization \begin{equation}\label{afqrgqrb} \omega_C(p_1+\ldots+p_n)=L\otimes\tilde L, \tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation} into two line bundles, $L$ of degree $d$ and $\tilde L$ of degree $\tilde d$ (and $d+\tilde d=2g-2+n$). %In the stable case one needs to further specify a vector of degrees on irreducible components of $C$. The~choice of $L$ is a choice of a point in $\operatorname{Pic}^dC$ and, when $C$ is smooth, every connected component of the Picard group has a uniform probability measure, the volume form invariant under translations by the subgroup $\operatorname{Pic}^0C$ of topologically trivial line bundles. %\footnote{ If~$C$ is not smooth then $\operatorname{Pic}^0C$ contains non-compact factors~$\Bbb C^*$ and probabilistic interpretation is less straightforward. In fact the case most related to physics literature is when all irreducible components of $C$ are rational curves and $\operatorname{Pic}^0C\simeq(\Bbb C^*)^g$. Non-compactness will not be an issue for us. %} \end{Review}
definitions: Let $C$ be a stable projective algebraic curve with marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_n$.

translation_statement_schema.claim: The probability distribution of residues for a random process giving a vector of log forms on (C; p_1, ..., p_n) is determined by the random tensor product factorization ω_C(p_1 + ... + p_n) = L ⊗ L̃, where L is chosen uniformly from Pic^d C with a volume form invariant under translations by Pic^0 C.
translation_statement_schema.theorem_intent: To determine the probability distribution of residues for a random process involving log forms on a curve with marked points.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
The second step is a choice of sections $s_\alpha\in H^0(C,L)$, $\tilde s_{\tilde\alpha}\in H^0(C,\tilde L)$
for some indices 
$\alpha\in I$ and 
$\tilde\alpha\in\tilde I$.
Tensoring these sections gives a matrix of log forms
$$\omega_{\alpha\tilde\alpha}=s_\alpha\otimes \tilde s_{\tilde\alpha}\in H^0(C,\omega_C(p_1+\ldots+p_n)).$$
We focus on the case $|I|=|\tilde I|=2$, which is  related to the $\Cal N=4$ Yang--Mills theory.
Let~$\Bbb L\subset \operatorname{Mat}_{2,2}$ be the subvariety of matrices of rank at most $1$.
The projectivization  $\Bbb P(\Bbb L)$ is a quadric in $\Bbb P^3$ isomorphic to $\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb P^1$.
Let
$$Y=\{\bold p_1,\ldots,\bold p_n\in\Bbb L\quad|\quad \bold p_1+\ldots+\bold p_n=0\}\subset \Bbb L^n.$$
The space $\operatorname{Mat}_{2,2}$ can be viewed as the complexified $4$-dimensional Minkowski space 
and $\Bbb L$ as the complexified light cone.
%The~complexified Lorentz group $G=\operatorname{SL}_2\times\operatorname{SL}_2$
%acts on $\operatorname{Mat}_{2,2}$ by left and right multiplication preserving $\Bbb L$.
Thus $Y$ parametrizes complexified momenta of $n$ massless particles in $4$ dimensions
satisfying the momentum conservation law.
We observe that  
$\left(\operatorname{Res}_{p_1}\omega_{\alpha\tilde\alpha},\ldots,\operatorname{Res}_{p_n}\omega_{\alpha\tilde\alpha}\right)\in Y$, since
each matrix 
$\operatorname{Res}_{p_i}\omega_{\alpha\tilde\alpha}$ is proportional  to the matrix $(s_\alpha(p_i)\tilde s_{\tilde\alpha}(p_i))$
after choosing trivializations.
So this matrix has rank at most one and  the residues of $\omega_{\alpha\tilde\alpha}$
(at marked points and nodes) can be viewed as momenta of massless (external and internal) particles.

This construction is reversible, at least in the most physically relevant case.
Namely, suppose all components of $C$ are rational and the dual graph is $3$-valent.
Given massless momenta  $\bold p_1,\ldots,\bold p_n$ (external) and  $\bold q_1,\ldots,\bold q_r$ (internal)
satisfying momentum conservation laws (one for each vertex of the dual graph), suppose
 that no two momenta adjacent to the same vertex are proportional.
Then there exists a factorization \eqref{afqrgqrb} and
sections $s_\alpha\in H^0(C,L)$, $\tilde s_{\tilde\alpha}\in H^0(C,\tilde L)$ for $\alpha,\tilde\alpha=1,2$
such that the momenta are  the residues of the $2\times 2$ matrix of log forms $(s_\alpha\otimes \tilde s_{\tilde\alpha})$.\footnote{Indeed, let $(\omega_{\alpha\tilde\alpha})$ be  the $2\times 2$ matrix of log forms that corresponds to the momenta.
Since the restriction of $\omega_C(p_1+\ldots+p_n)$ to every irreducible component of $C$ has degree $1$,
the sections $\omega_{\alpha\tilde\alpha}$ globally generate the line bundle $\omega_C(p_1+\ldots+p_n)$ and therefore
give a morphism $C\to\Bbb P^3$ such that every irreducible component of $C$ maps to a line. By assumption, images of marked points and nodes belong to the quadric
$\Bbb P(\Bbb L)$. Since a line intersecting a quadric in $3$ different points belongs to it, the image of $C$ lies on the quadric, i.e.~the matrix of log forms has rank~$1$.
The line bundles $L$ and $\tilde L$ are pull-backs of $\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb P^1}(1,0)$ 
and $\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb P^1}(0,1)$, where $\Bbb P(\Bbb L)\cong \Bbb P^1\times\Bbb P^1$.}

Let $$Y=\{\bold p_1,\ldots,\bold p_n\in\Bbb L\quad|\quad \bold p_1+\ldots+\bold p_n=0\}\subset \Bbb L^n.$$ The space $\operatorname{Mat}_{2,2}$ can be viewed as the complexified $4$-dimensional Minkowski space and $\Bbb L$ as the complexified light cone.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
We would like to focus on situations where there are no constraints on external and internal momenta,
i.e.~when a dense subset of points of $Y$ can be obtained as residues up to a finite ambiguity.
This requires imposing the following condition:\footnote{
By the Riemann--Roch theorem,
$H^0(C, L)$ and $H^0(C,\tilde L)$ have expected dimensions
$r=d+1-g$ and $\tilde r=\tilde d+1-g$.
Rescaling all $s_\alpha$ by $z\in\Bbb C^*$ and $\tilde s_{\tilde \alpha}$ by $z^{-1}$
gives the same forms $\omega_{\alpha\tilde\alpha}$ and so the same residues.
Thus the variety of choices $(L,\tilde L,s_\alpha,s_{\tilde \alpha})$ modulo $\Bbb C^*$ 
has expected dimension
$g+2r+2\tilde r-1=g+2n-1$. On the other hand, $Y$ is a variety of dimension $3n-4$
(assuming $n\ge 4$).
Thus the condition on dimensions is $g+2n-1=3n-4$, which is equivalent to \eqref{mnabdvfb}.
}
\begin{equation}\label{mnabdvfb}
n=g+3.
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
We would like to focus on situations where there are no constraints on external and internal momenta,
i.e.~when a dense subset of points of $Y$ can be obtained as residues up to a finite ambiguity.
This requires imposing the following condition:\footnote{
By the Riemann--Roch theorem,
$H^0(C, L)$ and $H^0(C,\tilde L)$ have expected dimensions
$r=d+1-g$ and $\tilde r=\tilde d+1-g$.
Rescaling all $s_\alpha$ by $z\in\Bbb C^*$ and $\tilde s_{\tilde \alpha}$ by $z^{-1}$
gives the same forms $\omega_{\alpha\tilde\alpha}$ and so the same residues.
Thus the variety of choices $(L,\tilde L,s_\alpha,s_{\tilde \alpha})$ modulo $\Bbb C^*$ 
has expected dimension
$g+2r+2\tilde r-1=g+2n-1$. On the other hand, $Y$ is a variety of dimension $3n-4$
(assuming $n\ge 4$).
Thus the condition on dimensions is $g+2n-1=3n-4$, which is equivalent to \eqref{mnabdvfb}.
}
\begin{equation}\label{mnabdvfb}
n=g+3.
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
We would like to study distribution of  residues of the matrix $\omega_{\alpha\tilde\alpha}$ 
but  we run into a  problem: there is no
natural probability measure 
on the space of sections $s_\alpha$, $\tilde s_{\tilde\alpha}$ unless we choose some extra data
such as a hermitian metric on $L$.
%Metaphorically speaking, a line bundle~$L$~is~a~``known unknown'', a random quantity with a known probability distribution
%while its section $s_\alpha$ is an ``unknown unknown'', a random quantity with an unknown probability distribution.
Instead of making a choice of a specific 
probability measure, we can design an experiment with outcomes 
that don't depend on sections
%``unknown unknowns''. 
by introducing a rational  (i.e.~defined on an open subset) map
$\Lambda:\,Y\dashrightarrow M$
to some algebraic variety $M$.
If $\Lambda$ is independent of sections $s_\alpha$ and $\tilde s_{\tilde\alpha}$
then it descends to a rational map 
${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^dC\dashrightarrow M$
and describing probability measure on $M$ becomes a well-posed problem.
Concretely, projecting the quadric $\Bbb P(\Bbb L)\cong\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb P^1$ to the first or the second factor gives rational maps 
$\Bbb L^n\dashrightarrow(\Bbb P^1)^n$ known as spinor variables in physics.
Taking a quotient by the $\operatorname{PGL}_2$ action gives a rational map $(\Bbb P^1)^n\dashrightarrow M_{0,n}$ to
the moduli space of $n$ distinct marked points on $\Bbb P^1$.
To summarize, we have two rational maps
$$\Lambda,  \tilde\Lambda:\, Y\hookrightarrow\Bbb L^n\dashrightarrow(\Bbb P^1)^n\dashrightarrow M_{0,n}.$$
The image of a point 
$\left(\operatorname{Res}_{p_1}\omega_{\alpha\tilde\alpha},\ldots,\operatorname{Res}_{p_n}\omega_{\alpha\tilde\alpha}\right)$
under the map $\Lambda$ (resp.,  $\tilde\Lambda$)
is given by an $n$-tuple $([s_1(p_i):s_2(p_i)])_{i=1\ldots,n}$ 
(resp.,~$([\tilde s_1(p_i):\tilde s_2(p_i)])_{i=1\ldots,n}$) of points in $\Bbb P^1$ modulo $\operatorname{PGL}_2$.
One can further  compactify $M_{0,n}$ by the Grothendieck--Knudsen 
moduli space $\overline{M}_{0,n}$ of stable rational curves or choose a different copactification.

%To summarize,  
We start with a stable  curve $C$ of genus $g$ with $n=g+3$ marked points.
The~MHV experiment is a choice of a random line bundle $L$ of degree $d=g+1$ or,
equivalently, a random meromorphic function $\varphi_L:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$ of degree~$d$.
When $C$ is smooth, 
the probability distribution of $L$ is uniform with respect to the translation-invariant volume form on the Jacobian.
We study probability measure on $M_{0,n}$
that gives statistics of points $\varphi_L(p_1),\ldots,\varphi_L(p_n)\in\Bbb P^1$.

%Every smooth curve is an MHV curve (Theorem~\ref{efvwb}) and we get
a family of (complexified) probability measures that depend on $4g$ complex parameters
describing the input curve $(C, p_1, ..., p_{n})\in\overline{M}_{g,n}$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{asgsrgr}
An MHV curve of genus $0$ is just $\Bbb P^1$ with three marked points. In~this case $d=1$ and $\operatorname{Pic}^1C$ is a point, namely the line bundle $L=\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}(1)$.
The~map $\varphi_L:\,\Bbb P^1\to\Bbb P^1$ is an isomorphism 
which maps $p_1,p_2,p_3$ to three distinct points, which can be moved to the points $0,1,\infty$ by applying the $\operatorname{PGL}_2$ action.
It~follows that the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}$ in genus $0$ is simply
$$\operatorname{pt}=\operatorname{Pic}^1\Bbb P^1\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{{\bold\Lambda}} M_{0,3}=\operatorname{pt}.$$

theorem rbqerhqer (n : ℕ) :
  d = 2 ∧ n = 4

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{rbqerhqer}
In genus $1$ there are many possibilities illustrated in Figure~\ref{SDgSg}. 
Here $d=2$ and $n=4$. So the map $\varphi_L:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$ is a double cover 
and~the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}$ takes $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^2C$ to the cross-ratio of four points 
$$\varphi_L(p_1),\ \varphi_L(p_2),\ \varphi_L(p_3),\ \varphi_L(p_4)\in\Bbb P^1.$$
It turns out that if $C$ is a smooth elliptic curve then ${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^2C\to\Bbb P^1$ is  a double cover
and the scattering amplitude form $A$ is an integrand  of an elliptic integral.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Contents of~\ref{khgckhfckhfc}] 
One of the goals of the paper is to {\em localize}  $2^g$ line bundles
in the preimage of a general point of $M_{0,n}$,
i.e. to describe branches of the multi-valued inverse function~${\bold\Lambda}^{-1}$.
In Theorem~\ref{efvwb} we  show that every smooth curve  is an MHV curve.
We use the theory of special divisors \cite{ACGH}, which was classically developed to 
study the images of Abel--Jacobi maps, in particular the focus was on the $d=g-1$ case.
The MHV regime $d=g+1$ is just as rich and exciting.
We~introduce various special divisors in $\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$  as well as the {\em planar locus} $W$  that parametrizes presentations of the curve $C$
as a nodal plane curve of degree $g+1$.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{Review}[Contents of~\ref{khgckhfckhfc}] One of the goals of the paper is to {\em localize} $2^g$ line bundles in the preimage of a general point of $M_{0,n}$, i.e. to describe branches of the multi-valued inverse function~${\bold\Lambda}^{-1}$. In Theorem~\ref{efvwb} we show that every smooth curve is an MHV curve. We use the theory of special divisors \cite{ACGH}, which was classically developed to study the images of Abel--Jacobi maps, in particular the focus was on the $d=g-1$ case. The MHV regime $d=g+1$ is just as rich and exciting. We~introduce various special divisors in $\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$ as well as the {\em planar locus} $W$ that parametrizes presentations of the curve $C$ as a nodal plane curve of degree $g+1$. \end{Review}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Contents of~\ref{zdfbsdh}]
Given that all smooth curves are MHV curves, one expects a simple classification of stable MHV curves
but this is quite a delicate question, which we start to investigate in this section.
These results are not central to the paper but they are used throughout.
If~the curve $C$ has a node such that removing it separates $C$ into two connected components (i.e.~the on-shell diagram
is not $2$-connected) then $C$ is not an MHV curve -- the only MHV curves with compact Jacobians
are smooth curves.
This~is  known as a {\em one-channel factorization} in physics.
If the on-shell diagram is $2$-connected but not $3$-connected 
(more precisely, if $C$ has a two-channel factorization,
see Definition~\ref{FbfhFHF}) then
$C$ is separated into two components by removing two nodes, and
information about the scattering amplitude can be read from the components, see
Theorems~\ref{safbsfhsfn} and~\ref{ADvAeg}.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Contents of~\ref{zdadthethfbsdh}] 
To study families of MHV curves and scattering amplitudes,
we 
introduce the 
universal
scattering amplitude map 
$${\bold\Lambda}:\ \operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}\Cal C\dashrightarrow M_{0,n}$$
over the
locus of MHV curves $\overline{\Cal M}^{\operatorname{MHV}}_{g,n}\subset\overline{\Cal M}_{g,n}$.
We use two open substacks in the stack of quasi-maps: moduli of
stable quotients \cite{MOP} and moduli of presentations of slope-stable line bundles.
We find a convenient polarization for MHV curves
and compactify $\operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}\Cal C$ by a projective family %$\overline{\cPic}^{MHV}\Cal C$
of compactified Jacobians.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Example~\ref{rbqerhqer} --  continued]
While a smooth elliptic curve 
$E$  degenerates into a wheel $C$ of {\em four} projective lines,  $\operatorname{Pic}^2E$ 
degenerates into $\overline{\Pic}^{MHV}C$, a wheel of {\em two} projective lines, the  MHV components $\operatorname{Pic}^{0,1,0,1}$ and $\operatorname{Pic}^{1,0,1,0}$ 
represented by stacked on-shell diagrams on the right side of Figure~\ref{SDgSg}.
Each of these components has the same  amplitude form,
a phenomenon called {\em ``square move''} by physicists.
For any of the curves in Figure ~\ref{SDgSg},
the fibre of the universal scattering amplitude map is 
a ramified double cover of $\Bbb P^1$ by an irreducible curve of arithmetic genus $1$ 
except for the four-wheel, when it becomes a reducible $2:1$ cover 
$$\overline{\Pic}^{MHV}C=\Bbb P^1\cup\Bbb P^1\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\Bbb P^1=\overline{M}_{0,4}.$$

represented by stacked on-shell diagrams on the right side of Figure~\ref{SDgSg}.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Contents of~\ref{sdvqefv}] 
Scattering amplitude maps and forms  of hyperelliptic curves give a new perspective on the theory of parabolic vector bundles of rank~$2$ on~$\Bbb P^1$.
A~hyperelliptic curve $C$ is given by the equation $y^2=f(z)$, where $f$ is a polynomial of degree $2g+2$ or $2g+1$ without multiple roots.
This gives a double cover map 
$$\varphi_h:\,C\to\Bbb P^1,\quad  (z,y)\mapsto z.$$
Marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_n$ project to points $z_1,\ldots,z_n\in\Bbb P^1$, which we assume are different.
In the study of pointed hyperelliptic curves it is often assumed that all marked points are Weierstrass points (the roots of $f(z)$)
but in our approach the marked points are  decoupled from the Weierstrass points.
We show in 
Lemma~\ref{SSRHS}
that the scattering amplitude map of hyperelliptic curves
factors as follows:
$${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{\overline{\bold\Lambda}} \operatorname{Bun}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)\mathop{\dashrightarrow}\limits^{\Xi} M_{0,n},$$
where $\operatorname{Bun}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$ is the moduli stack of parabolic rank $2$ bundles with trivial determinant,
the map $\overline{\bold\Lambda}$ associates to a line bundle $L\in \operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$ its push-forward $(\varphi_h)_*L$
with parabolic lines determined by marked points, and finally $\Xi$ 
is a  very basic birational map 
which can be described as follows. A projectivization of a generic parabolic vector bundle is a ruled surface $\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb P^1$ with points 
$(z_1,q_1),\ldots,(z_n,q_n)$ giving the parabolic structure and the map $\Xi$  assigns $(q_1,\ldots,q_n)$ to this bundle, see Figure~\ref{shasrhar}.
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\includegraphics[width=2.6in]{PicD.pdf}
\caption{$\Xi:\,\operatorname{Bun}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)\dashrightarrow M_{0,n}$, \quad $(E;V_1,\ldots,V_n)\mapsto (q_1,\ldots,q_n).$}\label{shasrhar}
\end{figure}
It~follows that in the hyperelliptic case the scattering amplitude map
combines effects of the birational morphism $\Xi$, which only depends on $z_1,\ldots,z_n$
but not on $C$, and the map~$\overline{\bold\Lambda}$, which is thus a finer invariant in the hyperelliptic case.
To study the scattering amplitude form as a form on the moduli space of parabolic bundles,
we have to choose its  projective model.
We study effects of a well-known wall-crossing between  projective models
and the action of the Weyl group $W(D_n)$ by elementary transformations.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Contents of~\ref{kwJHEfg}] 
In Theorem~\ref{asfgbsfgasfh} we make another step towards proving Theorem~\ref{2^gTheorem}
- we show that the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}$
has degree~$2^g$ for every hyperelliptic curve $C$.
We~use beautiful results of Jacobi \cite{Jacobi} (amplified by Moser and Mumford~\cite{Mumford}) that give an explicit
model for the Jacobian of a hyperelliptic curve (with a removed theta-divisor)
as the orbit space of conjugacy classes of $2\times 2$ polynomial matrices.
Moreover, translation-invariant vector 
fields on the Jacobian can be described in the form of the Lax differential equation
and this can be used 
to compute branches of the scattering amplitude form.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Contents of~\ref{sfgasgsRH}] 
In genus $2$, where every curve is hyperelliptic, we combine methods of \ref{sdvqefv} 
with a classical observation of Halphen \cite{Halphen}: 
a sufficiently general divisor $P=p_1+\ldots+p_n$ of marked points  on a smooth MHV curve
embeds~it
$\varphi_{P}:\,C\hookrightarrow \Bbb P^3$
as a degree $n=g+3$ space curve. 
%In genus~$2$ this works for any choice of~$P$. 
%Moreover, the scattering
%amplitude map in genus $2$ is a function of a degree $3$ divisor, which can be realized as a residual divisor of the pencil
%of planes in $\Bbb P^3$ passing through two  points of~$C$. 
This~gives a way to study the scattering amplitude 
using geometry of $\Bbb P^3$.
By the results of  \ref{sdvqefv},
the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^3C\dashrightarrow \overline{M}_{0,5}\simeq \bold{dP}_5$
into the~quintic del Pezzo surface
factors through the moduli space of parabolic vector bundles of rank~$2$, which in this case is the quartic del Pezzo surface~$\bold{dP}_4$.
In~Theorem~\ref{vasdgsgG} we resolve this map by a finite morphism $\overline{\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Bl}_{16}\operatorname{Pic}^3C\to\bold{dP}_4$
of degree $4$ 
from the blow-up of $\operatorname{Pic}^3C$ in $16$ special points
to the quartic del Pezzo surface.
We~show in Theorem~\ref{sDGSG}  that whenever all marked points are Weierstrass points,
$\overline{\bold\Lambda}$~becomes a well-known map: 
it factors as a composition of two double covers, 
$$\operatorname{Bl}_{16}\operatorname{Pic}^3C\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}  \hbox{\rm K3}\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\bold{dP}_4,$$
where the K3 surface is the minimal resolution of the Kummer surface.
For general marked points away from the Weierstrass points, the intermediate K3 surface disappears from the picture 
but the degree~$4$ scattering amplitude morphism $\overline{\bold\Lambda}$  as well as the
{\em double sixteen} configuration  on the abelian surface survive.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Contents of~\ref{sdcqwv}] In this section we inject a measure of reality
into the study of scattering amplitude forms of smooth curves. In order to obtain real probability measures, we have
to turn to real algebraic geometry.
The answer is beautiful for
smooth real curves with the maximal number of real ovals, so called M-curves,
with marked points distributed as in Figure~\ref{wEGsg}: 
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centerline{\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{TypeAB.pdf}}
\caption{MHV M-curves of type A (left) and type B (right)}\label{wEGsg}
\end{figure}
either all marked points are real and all ovals contain one marked point except for one that contains three (type~A)
or all but two marked points are real, one for each real oval, and the other two are complex-conjugate
(type B). The main result of this section is Theorem~\ref{dfvwevev}:
line bundles in every fiber of the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}$
over $M_{0,n}(\Bbb R)$
``localize'' into different connected components (there are conveniently exactly $2^g$ of them)
$$\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}_I(\Bbb R)\subset \operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}(\Bbb R).$$ 
We use this theorem of real algebraic geometry
to prove Theorem~\ref{2^gTheorem}, which is a theorem of complex algebraic geometry.
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.55\textwidth]{results1B.pdf}\qquad
\includegraphics[width=0.35\textwidth]{results1C.pdf}}
\caption{Scattering amplitude probability measures in genus $2$}\label{kgfkhgfh}
\end{figure}
Back to M-curves, in Theorem~\ref{DSvSDb} we show that the scattering amplitude map 
${\bold\Lambda}$ gives real-analytic open immersions 
$${\bold\Lambda}_I:\,\operatorname{Pic}_I^{g+1}(\Bbb R)\hookrightarrow M_{0,n}(\Bbb R)$$
from an open dense subset of every connected component of  $\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$.
This gives real probability measures $|A_I|$ on $M_{0,n}(\Bbb R)$ that depend on $4g$ real parameters.
For an especially nice connected component $\operatorname{Pic}_H^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$,
which we call the {\em Huisman component}, 
the scattering amplitude map induces a real-analytic isomorphism
$$\Bbb R^g/\Bbb Z^g\simeq\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}_H(\Bbb R)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{\bold\Lambda}(\Bbb R\Bbb P^1)^g,$$
which gives a positive, smooth (in fact real-analytic) scattering amplitude probability measure on $(\Bbb R\Bbb P^1)^g$, see Theorem~\ref{sGARSGARHA}.
As an example, we study scattering amplitude probability measures in genus $2$,
which produces pretty probability density functions as in Figure~\ref{kgfkhgfh}.

The answer is beautiful for smooth real curves with the maximal number of real ovals, so called M-curves, with marked points distributed as in Figure~\ref{wEGsg}: \begin{figure}[htbp] \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{TypeAB.pdf}} \caption{MHV M-curves of type A (left) and type B (right)}\label{wEGsg} \end{figure} either all marked points are real and all ovals contain one marked point except for one that contains three (type~A) or all but two marked points are real, one for each real oval, and the other two are complex-conjugate (type B). The main result of this section is Theorem~\ref{dfvwevev}: line bundles in every fiber of the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}$ over $M_{0,n}(\Bbb R)$ ``localize'' into different connected components (there are conveniently exactly $2^g$ of them) $$\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}_I(\Bbb R)\subset \operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}(\Bbb R).$$ We use this theorem of real algebraic geometry to prove Theorem~\ref{2^gTheorem}, which is a theorem of complex algebraic geometry. \begin{figure}[htbp] \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.55\textwidth]{results1B.pdf}\qquad \includegraphics[width=0.35\textwidth]{results1C.pdf}} \caption{Scattering amplitude probability measures in genus $2$}\label{kgfkhgfh} \end{figure} Back to M-curves, in Theorem~\ref{DSvSDb} we show that the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}$ gives real-analytic open immersions $${\bold\Lambda}_I:\,\operatorname{Pic}_I^{g+1}(\Bbb R)\hookrightarrow M_{0,n}(\Bbb R)$$ from an open dense subset of every connected component of $\opera

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
Let $C$ be a smooth genus $1$  curve with $4$ marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_4$.
Every line bundle $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^2C$ gives a double cover 
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\includegraphics[height=2in]{elli4.pdf}
\end{figure}
$\varphi_L:\,C\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\Bbb P^1$
presenting $C$ in the form $y^2=f(x)$, where $f$ is a polynomial of degree $3$ or~$4$.
Recall that 
$$M_{0,4}\simeq\Bbb P^1\setminus\{0,1,\infty\}$$ via the cross-ratio function of four points.
We~compactify $M_{0,4}$ by $\overline{M}_{0,4}\simeq\Bbb P^1$.
If~we denote by $q_1,\ldots,q_4$ the images of marked points $\varphi_L(p_1),\ldots,\varphi_L(p_4)$
then the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}$ takes $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^2C$ to their cross-ratio function
$${q_4-q_1\over q_2-q_1}\cdot {q_2-q_3\over q_4-q_3}.$$
Of course $\operatorname{Pic}^2 C\simeq C$ but not canonically. In~fact $\operatorname{Pic}^2C$ has
$6$ distinguished points 
$$p_{ij}=\Cal O(p_i+p_j),\quad i\ne j,$$
and  carries a natural degree $2$ line bundle
$$\Cal L=\Cal O(p_{12}+p_{34})\simeq\Cal O(p_{13}+p_{24})\simeq\Cal O(p_{14}+p_{23}).$$
The images of points $p_{ij}\in\operatorname{Pic}^2 C$ under the scattering amplitude map are
$${\bold\Lambda}(p_{14})={\bold\Lambda}(p_{23})=0,\quad {\bold\Lambda}(p_{13})={\bold\Lambda}(p_{24})=1,\quad {\bold\Lambda}(p_{12})={\bold\Lambda}(p_{34})=\infty$$
and   ${\bold\Lambda}$ is the  double cover 
$$\varphi_{\Cal L}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^2C\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\Bbb P^1.$$ 
In the complex torus model $\operatorname{Pic}^2C=\Bbb C/\Bbb Z+\Bbb Z\tau$, 
the  inverse of the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}$ is given by the elliptic integral
$$z=\int_{x_0}^x {dx \over \sqrt{g(x)}},$$
where $g$ is a degree $4$ or $3$ polynomial. To summarize:
\begin{enumerate}
\item The scattering amplitude form $A(x)={\displaystyle dx \over\displaystyle \sqrt{g(x)}}$ has
$4$ branch points. 
\item The curve $C$ is uniquely determined by the scattering amplitude form.
\item Marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_4$ are determined by ${\bold\Lambda}^{-1}(0)$, ${\bold\Lambda}^{-1}(1)$ and ${\bold\Lambda}^{-1}(\infty)$ 
but not uniquely: the action of the Klein $4$-group that permutes the marked points  $p_1,\ldots,p_4$ in pairs is not detected by the scattering amplitude.
\end{enumerate}

$$M_{0,4}\simeq\Bbb P^1\setminus\{0,1,\infty\}$$ via the cross-ratio function of four points.
We~compactify $M_{0,4}$ by $\overline{M}_{0,4}\simeq\Bbb P^1$.

Every line bundle $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^2C$ gives a double cover \begin{figure}[htbp] \includegraphics[height=2in]{elli4.pdf} \end{figure} $\varphi_L:\,C\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\Bbb P^1$ presenting $C$ in the form $y^2=f(x)$, where $f$ is a polynomial of degree $3$ or~$4$.

In~fact $\operatorname{Pic}^2C$ has $6$ distinguished points $$p_{ij}=\Cal O(p_i+p_j),\quad i\ne j,$$ and carries a natural degree $2$ line bundle $$\Cal L=\Cal O(p_{12}+p_{34})\simeq\Cal O(p_{13}+p_{24})\simeq\Cal O(p_{14}+p_{23}).$$ The images of points $p_{ij}\in\operatorname{Pic}^2 C$ under the scattering amplitude map are $${\bold\Lambda}(p_{14})={\bold\Lambda}(p_{23})=0,\quad {\bold\Lambda}(p_{13})={\bold\Lambda}(p_{24})=1,\quad {\bold\Lambda}(p_{12})={\bold\Lambda}(p_{34})=\infty$$ and ${\bold\Lambda}$ is the double cover $$\varphi_{\Cal L}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^2C\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\Bbb P^1.$$ In the complex torus model $\operatorname{Pic}^2C=\Bbb C/\Bbb Z+\Bbb Z\tau$, the inverse of the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}$ is given by the elliptic integral $$z=\int_{x_0}^x {dx \over \sqrt{g(x)}},$$ where $g$ is a degree $4$ or $3$ polynomial.
translation_statement_schema.claim: Recall that $$M_{0,4}\simeq\Bbb P^1\setminus\{0,1,\infty\}$$ via the cross-ratio function of four points. We~compactify $M_{0,4}$ by $\overline{M}_{0,4}\simeq\Bbb P^1$. entries.lean_statement:
theorem zxbzfn
  {C : Type*} [AddCommGroup C] [Module ℝ C] [TopologicalSpace C] [T2Space C]
  (g : ℕ) (p : Fin (g + 3) → C)
  (W E : Set (Pic C)) (Eij : Fin n → Fin n → Set (Pic C))
  (R Θ : Set (Pic C))
  (hW : W = {L | ∃ x : Fin (g - 3) → C, L = Pic.map (fun i => omega_C - ∑ j, x j)})
  (hE : E = {L | ∃ p' : C × Fin (g - 2) → C, L = Pic.map (fun i => omega_C - p'.1 - ∑ j, p'.2 j)})
  (hEij : ∀ i j, i < j → Eij i j = {L | ∃ x : Fin (g - 1) → C, L = Pic.map (fun k => omega_C - ∑ l, x l + p i + p j)})
  (hΘ : Θ = {L | ∃ x : Fin (g - 1) → C, L = Pic.map (fun i => ∑ j, x j + ∑ k, p k)})
  (hR : R = {L | L ^ 2 ∈ Θ})
  : (∀ L, L ∉ W → ¬IsSpecial L) ∧
    (∀ L, L ∉ E → BasePointFree L) ∧
    (∀ L, L ∉ E ∧ ∀ i j, i < j → L ∉ Eij i j → Λ L ∈ M0n) ∧
    (∀ L, L ∉ E ∧ ∀ i j, i < j → L ∉ Eij i j → L ∉ R → Unramified Λ L) ∧
    (Codimension W (Pic C) = 3) ∧
    (∀ i j, i < j → IsThetaDivisor (Eij i j)) ∧
    (IsThetaDivisor E ∨ E ≡ (g - 1) • Θ) ∧
    (AlgebraicEquiv R (4 • Θ))

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[planar locus $W$ and special divisors $E, E_{ij}, R\subset \operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$]\label{zxbzfn} $\ $
\begin{enumerate}
\item $L$ is not special away from the image $W$ of the map 
$$\operatorname{Sym}^{g-3}C\to\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C,\quad (x_1,\ldots,x_{g-3}) \mapsto \omega_C(-x_1-\ldots-x_{g-3}).$$
\item $L$ is base-point-free away from the image $E$ of the map 
$$C\times\operatorname{Sym}^{g-2}C\to\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C,\quad (p,x_1,\ldots,x_{g-2}) \mapsto \omega_C(p-x_1-\ldots-x_{g-2}).$$
\item ${\bold\Lambda}(L)\in M_{0,n}$ when $L$ is away from $E$ and  the images $E_{ij}$ for 
$1\le i<j\le n$ of the maps $\operatorname{Sym}^{g-1}C\to\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$,
$$(x_1,\ldots,x_{g-1}) \mapsto \omega_C(-x_1-\ldots-x_{g-1}+p_i+p_j).$$
\item 
Let $\Theta\subset\operatorname{Pic}^{2g+2}$  be the image of the map $\operatorname{Sym}^{g-1}C\to\operatorname{Pic}^{2g+2}C$,
$$(x_1,\ldots,x_{g-1}) \mapsto \Cal O(x_1+\ldots+x_{g-1}+p_1+\ldots+p_{g+3}).$$
The scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}$ is unramified away from $E$, $E_{ij}$ and the locus $R=m^{-1}(\Theta)$,
where $m$ is the map 
$$m:\,\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C\to\operatorname{Pic}^{2g+2}C,\quad L\mapsto L^{\otimes 2}.$$
\item $W\subset E\cap\bigcap\limits_{i\ne j} E_{ij}$ has codimension $3$ in $\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1} C$. 
\item  
$E_{ij}$ and $\Theta$ are theta divisors.
\item $E$ is a theta divisor if $C$ is hyperelliptic, otherwise $E\equiv (g-1)\Theta$.
\item The divisor $R$ is algebraically equivalent to $4\Theta$.
\end{enumerate}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Loci $E$ and $E_{ij}$]
After choosing a compactification of $M_{0,n}$, for example $\overline{M}_{0,n}$, 
the scattering amplitude map (as any rational map) 
will extend generically along divisors $E$ and $E_{ij}$. Line bundles $L\in E$ have base locus but (away from $W$) 
still determine meromorphic functions $\varphi_L:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$. However, moving base points doesn't change $\varphi_L$
but changes $L$ and as a result ${\bold\Lambda}$ contracts $E$ to a locus of smaller dimension, 
most dramatically to a point $o\in M_{0,n}$ in the hyperelliptic case 
(see~Corollary~\ref{adfgadrha}). This produces a singularity in the scattering amplitude probability measure (see \ref{qergarh}).
By contrast, divisors $E_{ij}$ are largely harmless: extension of ${\bold\Lambda}$ maps $E_{ij}$  to the locus in the compactification of $M_{0,n}$
where two marked points in $\Bbb P^1$ come together, for example to the boundary divisor $\Delta_{ij}$ in the case of~$\overline{M}_{0,n}$.
Here is an example of a different and very ergonomic compactification.
semantic_equivalence_artifact.normalized_natural_language_theorem: [Loci E and E_ ij ] After choosing a compactification of M_ 0,n , for example overline M_ 0,n , the scattering amplitude map (as any rational map) will extend generically along divisors E and E_ ij . Line bundles Lin E have base locus but (away from W ) still determine meromorphic functions varphi_L:\,CtoBbb P^1 . However, moving base points doesn't change varphi_L but changes L and as a result boldLambda contracts E to a locus of smaller dimension, most dramatically to a point oin M_ 0,n in the hyperelliptic case (see~Corollary~ ). context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
[Loci $E$ and $E_{ij}$]
After choosing a compactification of $M_{0,n}$, for example $\overline{M}_{0,n}$, 
the scattering amplitude map (as any rational map) 
will extend generically along divisors $E$ and $E_{ij}$. Line bundles $L\in E$ have base locus but (away from $W$) 
still determine meromorphic functions $\varphi_L:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$. However, moving base points doesn't change $\varphi_L$
but changes $L$ and as a result ${\bold\Lambda}$ contracts $E$ to a locus of smaller dimension, 
most dramatically to a point $o\in M_{0,n}$ in the hyperelliptic case 
(see~Corollary~\ref{adfgadrha}). This produces a singularity in the scattering amplitude probability measure (see \ref{qergarh}).
By contrast, divisors $E_{ij}$ are largely harmless: extension of ${\bold\Lambda}$ maps $E_{ij}$  to the locus in the compactification of $M_{0,n}$
where two marked points in $\Bbb P^1$ come together, for example to the boundary divisor $\Delta_{ij}$ in the case of~$\overline{M}_{0,n}$.
Here is an example of a different and very ergonomic compactification.

Line bundles $L\in E$ have base locus but (away from $W$) still determine meromorphic functions $\varphi_L:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$. However, moving base points doesn't change $\varphi_L$ but changes $L$ and as a result ${\bold\Lambda}$ contracts $E$ to a locus of smaller dimension, most dramatically to a point $o\in M_{0,n}$ in the hyperelliptic case (see~Corollary~\ref{adfgadrha}). This produces a singularity in the scattering amplitude probability measure (see \ref{qergarh}). By contrast, divisors $E_{ij}$ are largely harmless: extension of ${\bold\Lambda}$ maps $E_{ij}$ to the locus in the compactification of $M_{0,n}$ where two marked points in $\Bbb P^1$ come together, for example to the boundary divisor $\Delta_{ij}$ in the case of~$\overline{M}_{0,n}$. Here is an example of a different and very ergonomic compactification. \end{Review}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{adrhadrhsth}
Fix three indices, for example $g+1,g+2,g+3$.
The scattering amplitude map induces a generically finite rational map
\begin{equation}\label{bSheb}
{\bold\Lambda}:\, \operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C\dashrightarrow M_{0,n}\xrightarrow{\pi_1,\ldots,\pi_g}(\overline{M}_{0,4})^g\simeq(\Bbb P^1)^g,
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
where %the $i$-th projection map 
$\pi_i:\,M_{0,n}\to \overline{M}_{0,4}$ for $i=1,\ldots,g$
is the forgetful morphism given by the indices $i,g+1,g+2,g+3$.
A general line bundle $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$ is mapped by $\pi_i$ to the cross-ratio
of points 
$\varphi_L(p_i),\varphi_L(p_{g+1}),\varphi_L(p_{g+2}),\varphi_L(p_{g+3})$.
Let $L\in \operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C\setminus\{R\cup E\}$. If points $\varphi_L(p_{g+1}),\varphi_L(p_{g+2}),\varphi_L(p_{g+3})$
are different then \eqref{bSheb} is regular and unramified at $L$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Locus W]\label{evwevwev}
We call $W$ the {\em planar locus}:
generically along $W$, $\varphi_L$ is a morphism 
\begin{equation}\label{sgasgas}
\varphi_L:\,C\to\Bbb P^2.
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
If $C$ is a general curve, $\varphi_L$ realizes it as a degree $g+1$ 
plane curve with ${g(g-3)\over 2}$ nodes away from the marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_n$.
Generically along $W$, the scattering amplitude map is resolved by the blow-up 
\begin{equation}\label{agargasr}
{\bold\Lambda}:\,G=\operatorname{Bl}_W\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C\dashrightarrow M_{0,n}.
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
In the language of 
Brill--Noether theory~\cite{ACGH}, $G$ parametrizes 
pencils of divisors on $C$ of degree $g+1$. Let 
$$\hat W\subset G$$
be an exceptional divisor over $W$. The map $\hat W\to W$ is a $\Bbb P^2$-bundle (generically along $W$).
Generically along $\hat W$, the map ${\bold\Lambda}$ of \eqref{agargasr} can be described as follows: 
a~point $(L,p)\in\hat W$ gives both a ``planar realization''
\eqref{sgasgas} and a  point $p\in\Bbb P^2$.
Projecting points 
$$\varphi_L(p_1),\ldots,\varphi_L(p_n)\in\Bbb P^2$$ from $p$
gives points $q_1,\ldots,q_n\in \Bbb P^1$.
The class of $(q_1,\ldots,q_n)$ in $M_{0,n}$
is the image of $(L,p)$ under~${\bold\Lambda}$. 
By \cite[Th.~3.1]{CT_Cont}, ${\bold\Lambda}(D_W)\subset\overline{M}_{0,n}$ is a divisor 
covered by surfaces 
\begin{equation}\label{aljskhfgsk}
S=\operatorname{Bl}_{\varphi_L(p_1),\ldots,\varphi_L(p_n)}\Bbb P^2
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
for general $L\in W$
unless points $\varphi_L(p_1),\ldots,\varphi_L(p_n)$ lie on a conic, in which case the conic is contracted to a point.

assumptions: Let $$\hat W\subset G$$ be an exceptional divisor over $W$.

By \cite[Th.~3.1]{CT_Cont}, ${\bold\Lambda}(D_W)\subset\overline{M}_{0,n}$ is a divisor covered by surfaces \begin{equation}\label{aljskhfgsk} S=\operatorname{Bl}_{\varphi_L(p_1),\ldots,\varphi_L(p_n)}\Bbb P^2 \tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation} for general $L\in W$ unless points $\varphi_L(p_1),\ldots,\varphi_L(p_n)$ lie on a conic, in which case the conic is contracted to a point.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
$W$ is empty in genus $1$ and $2$.
For a curve of genus $3$,
$$W=\{K\}\in\operatorname{Pic}^{4}C$$
and $\varphi_K:\,C\to\Bbb P^2$ is an embedding of $C$ as a quartic curve if $C$ is not hyperelliptic
and a $2:1$ map to a conic in $\Bbb P^2$ if $C$ is hyperelliptic.
If $C$ is a general quartic (resp. ~ hyperelliptic) curve then 
$S$ as in \eqref{aljskhfgsk} is a general smooth (resp.~nodal) cubic surface.
This is related to the fact that $M_{0,6}$ has another projective model, 
the Segre cubic threefold in $\Bbb P^4$, and $S$ is its hyperplane section.
At the moment little is known
about divisors ${\bold\Lambda}(\hat W)\subset M_{0,n}$ for $g>3$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{asrsrh}
Recall that  the on-shell diagram is the dual graph of $C$ decorated with degrees of $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C$ on its irreducible components.
If~$C$ is an MHV curve then none of these degrees are negative since $L$ is globally generated.
However, some of the degrees can be equal to $0$.
The corresponding components are 
contracted by the map $\varphi_L:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$.
The union of irreducible components where $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C$ has degree $0$ can be written 
as a disjoint union of maximal connected components
$$C^{(0)}_1,\ldots, C^{(0)}_r.$$
The following lemma is essentially from \cite{MHV}:

translation_statement_schema.claim: \label{asrsrh} Recall that the on-shell diagram is the dual graph of $C$ decorated with degrees of $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C$ on its irreducible components. context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{Lemma}\label{wefwetb} Let $(C, \vec d)$ be an MHV curve with a connected subcurve $A$ of arithmetic genus~$p$, and $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C$ a generic line bundle. \begin{enumerate} \item If $p>0$ then $\deg L|_A\ge p+1$. \item If $\deg L|_A=p+1$ then $A$ contains at most $p+3$ marked points. \end{enumerate} \end{Lemma}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{sDGASG}
Let $C$ be an irreducible nodal curve of arithmetic genus $1$  with $4$ marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_4$.
%\begin{figure}[htbp]
%\includegraphics[width=3.6in]{pic2.pdf}
%\end{figure}
We~view $C$ as $\Bbb P^1$ with $0$ and $\infty$ identified. Marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_4\in\Bbb C^*\subset \Bbb P^1$.
For $x,y\in\Bbb C^*$, the line bundle $L=\Cal O([x]+[y])\in\operatorname{Pic}^2C$ determines the map 
$$\varphi_L:\,C\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\Bbb P^1,\qquad p\mapsto {p\over (p-x)(p-y)}$$ 
(note that $\varphi_L(0)=\varphi_L(\infty)$).
It is easy to see that $\Cal O([x]+[y])\simeq \Cal O([x']+[y'])$ if and only if $xy=x'y'=z\in\Bbb C^*$.
This gives an identification of $\operatorname{Pic}^2C\simeq\Bbb C^*$. The map
$${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^2C\to\overline{M}_{0,4}\simeq\Bbb P^1,\qquad {\bold\Lambda}(L)=\lambda=
[{\varphi_L(p_1):\varphi_L(p_2);\varphi_L(p_3):\varphi_L(p_4)}]$$
is the cross-ratio of points varying with $L$. Using invariance of the cross-ratio,
$${\bold\Lambda}(L)=\left[{p_1\over (p_1-x)(p_1-y)}:{p_2\over (p_2-x)(p_2-y)};{p_3\over (p_3-x)(p_3-y)}:{p_4\over (p_4-x)(p_4-y)}\right]$$
$$=\left[{ (p_1-x)(p_1-y)\over p_1}:{(p_2-x)(p_2-y)\over p_2};{(p_3-x)(p_3-y)\over p_3}:{(p_4-x)(p_4-y)\over p_1}\right]$$
$$=\left[{ p_1-(x+y)+{z\over p_1}}:{p_2-(x+y)+{z\over p_2}};{p_3-(x+y)+{z\over p_3}}:{p_4-(x+y)+{z\over p_4}}\right]$$
$$=\left[{ p_1+{z\over p_1}}:{p_2+{z\over p_2}};{p_3+{z\over p_3}}:{p_4+{z\over p_4}}\right]=\lambda$$
Thus $\lambda={\bold\Lambda}(z)$ extends to a map $\Bbb P^1\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\Bbb P^1$ that sends $0, \infty$ to $[p_1:p_2;p_3:p_4]$.
The target $\Bbb P^1$ is the $\overline{M}_{0,4}$ and the source $\Bbb P^1$ is a new feature of the stable curve case, the normalization of the
{\em compactified Jacobian} $\overline{\operatorname{Pic}^2C}$,  in this case  a nodal cubic.
The~ $1$-form ${dz\over z}$ of $\overline{\operatorname{Pic}^2C}$ is invariant under the group action of $\Bbb C^* 
\cong\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec0} C$ on $\overline{\operatorname{Pic}^2C}$. As~a~form on $\overline{\operatorname{Pic}^2C}$, the amplitude 
has log poles at its boundary point
but as a form of $\lambda$ the pole is located at $[p_1:p_2;p_3:p_4]\not\in\{0,1,\infty\}$.
To summarize,


\begin{enumerate}
\item The scattering amplitude form is $A(\lambda)={d\lambda\over (\lambda-\lambda_0)\sqrt{f_2(\lambda)}}$,
with the log pole 
$$\lambda_0=[p_1:p_2;p_3:p_4]\not\in\{0,1,\infty\},$$
where $f_2$ is a polynomial of degree $2$ with roots ${\bold\Lambda}\left(\pm\sqrt{p_1p_2p_3p_4}\right)$.
\item The marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_4$ are determined modulo 
the action of the Klein $4$-group that permutes them in pairs.
Specifically, 
$${\bold\Lambda}^{-1}(0)=\{p_1p_4,p_2p_3\},\quad {\bold\Lambda}^{-1}(1)=\{p_1p_3,p_2p_4\},\quad {\bold\Lambda}^{-1}(\infty)=\{p_1p_2,p_3p_4\}.$$
\end{enumerate}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
Next we consider various situations when the curve $C$
can be separated into two connected components $A$ and $B$ by  nodes.
We will use the following notation:
\begin{enumerate}
\item $g_A$ and $g_B$ for the arithmetic genus of components $A$ and $B$.
\item $d_A$ and $d_B$ for the degree of $L_A=L|_A$ and $L_B=L|_B$. We have $d_A+d_B=d$.
\item $N_A$ and $N_B$ for the subsets of marked points on $A$ and $B$ and $n_A$~and~$n_B$
for their cardinalities.
We have $n_A+n_B=n$.
\end{enumerate}

theorem SfbSfb
  (C : Type*) [AddCommGroup C] [Module ℝ C] [TopologicalSpace C] [T2Space C]
  (p1 p2 p3 p4 : C) (L x x' a b a' b' z lam : ℝ)
  (h_C_glued : C = (ℝ × ℝ) ⊔ (ℝ × ℝ) / (0, 0) ~ (1, 1))
  (h_p1_first : p1 ∈ Set.range (fun t : ℝ => (t, 0)))
  (h_p2_first : p2 ∈ Set.range (fun t : ℝ => (t, 0)))
  (h_p3_second : p3 ∈ Set.range (fun t : ℝ => (t, 1)))
  (h_p4_second : p4 ∈ Set.range (fun t : ℝ => (t, 1)))
  (h_L_Pic : L ∈ Set.range (fun (x x' : ℝ) => x + x'))
  (h_L_decomp : L = x + x')
  (h_x_not_node : x ≠ 0 ∧ x ≠ 1)
  (h_x_prime_not_node : x' ≠ 0 ∧ x' ≠ 1)
  (h_a_eq : a = a')
  (h_bx_eq : b * x' = b' * x)
  (h_phi_L_regular : ∀ p ∈ C, ∃ U : Set C, IsOpen U ∧ p ∈ U ∧ ∃ f : U → ℝ, Continuous f ∧ f p = a + b / (p - x))
  (h_Pic_equiv : ∀ L1 L2 : C, L1 ∈ Set.range (fun (x x' : ℝ) => x + x') → L2 ∈ Set.range (fun (y y' : ℝ) => y + y') → (x / x' = y / y' ↔ L1 = L2))
  : lam = (z * p4 - p1) / (p2 - p1) * (p2 - z * p3) / (z * p4 - z * p3)

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{SfbSfb}
Let $(C;p_1,p_2,p_3,p_4)$ be a curve obtained by gluing two copies of $\Bbb P^1$ at $0$ and $\infty$,
with $p_1,p_2$ on the first component and $p_3,p_4$ on the second. 
This is an example of a two-channel factorization.
The MHV component of the Picard group is $\operatorname{Pic}^{1,1}C$.
Every line bundle $L\in \operatorname{Pic}^{1,1}C$ can be written as $\Cal O([x]+[x'])$, where $x$ is a point of the first component and $x'$ of the second 
(away from the nodes of $C$). Consider the map
$$\varphi_L:\,C\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\Bbb P^1,\quad p\mapsto\begin{cases} 
a+{b\over p-x} & \hbox{\rm on the first component,}\cr
a'+{b'\over p-x'} & \hbox{\rm on the second component,}\cr
\end{cases}$$
where $a=a'$ and $bx'=b'x$ so that $\varphi_L$ is regular.
It is easy to see that $\Cal O([x]+[x'])\simeq\Cal O([y]+[y'])$ if and only if ${x\over x'}={y\over y'}=z$.
This gives an identification $\operatorname{Pic}^{1,1}C\simeq\Bbb C^*$.

We are studying the scattering amplitude map
${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^{1,1}C\to\overline{M}_{0,4}\simeq\Bbb P^1$ given by 
the cross-ratio of points $\varphi_L(p_1),\ldots,\varphi_L(p_4)$ varying with $L$. Using invariance of the cross-ratio,
$$\lambda=\left[
a+{b\over p_1-x}:
a+{b\over p_2-x};
a+{b'\over p_3-x'}:
a+{b'\over p_4-x'}
\right]
$$
$$=\left[
{b\over p_1-x}:
{b\over p_2-x};
{b'\over p_3-x'}:
{b'\over p_4-x'}
\right]=\left[
{x\over p_1-x}:
{x\over p_2-x};
{x'\over p_3-x'}:
{x'\over p_4-x'}
\right]
$$
$$=\left[
{p_1\over x}:
{p_2\over x};
{p_3\over x'}:
{p_4\over x'}
\right]
=\left[
{p_1}:
{p_2};
{z p_3}:
{z p_4}
\right]
={z p_4-p_1\over p_2-p_1}\cdot {p_2-z p_3\over z p_4-z p_3}.
$$
As in Example~\ref{sDGASG}, we view the map ${\bold\Lambda}$ as the map from the compactified Jacobian:
$$\overline{\operatorname{Pic}^{1,1}C}=\Bbb P^1_z/_{\{ 0=\infty\}}\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1} \Bbb P^1_\lambda=\overline{M}_{0,4}.$$
The difference from Example~\ref{sDGASG} is that 
${\bold\Lambda}(0)={\bold\Lambda}(\infty)=\infty$. In particular, the scattering amplitude form, which  
is the $\operatorname{Pic}^{0,0} C$- invariant form $dz\over z$ 
written as a multivalued form of ~$\lambda$, has a log pole at $\infty$.
Explicitly, from the above, we have
\begin{equation}\label{afbfhafhdf}
\lambda z(p_2-p_1)(p_4-p_3)=(zp_4-p_1)(p_2-zp_3).
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
Solving \eqref{afbfhafhdf} for $z$ using quadratic formula gives,
after  algebraic manipulations,
$${dz\over z}=(p_1-p_2)(p_4-p_3){d\lambda\over\sqrt{f_2(\lambda)}},$$
where $f_2(\lambda)$ is the discriminant of this quadratic equation.

a+{b\over p-x} & \hbox{\rm on the first component,}\cr
a'+{b'\over p-x'} & \hbox{\rm on the second component,}\cr
\end{cases}$$
where $a=a'$ and $bx'=b'x$ so that $\varphi_L$ is regular.
It is easy to see that $\Cal O([x]+[x'])\simeq\Cal O([y]+[y'])$ if and only if ${x\over x'}={y\over y'}=z$.
This gives an identification $\operatorname{Pic}^{1,1}C\simeq\Bbb C^*$.

We are studying the scattering amplitude map
${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^{1,1}C\to\overline{M}_{0,4}\simeq\Bbb P^1$ given by 
the cross-ratio of points $\varphi_L(p_1),\ldots,\varphi_L(p_4)$ varying with $L$. Using invariance of the cross-ratio,
$$\lambda=\left[
a+{b\over p_1-x}:
a+{b\over p_2-x};
a+{b'\over p_3-x'}:
a+{b'\over p_4-x'}
\right]
$$
$$=\left[
{b\over p_1-x}:
{b\over p_2-x};
{b'\over p_3-x'}:
{b'\over p_4-x'}
\right]=\left[
{x\over p_1-x}:
{x\over p_2-x};
{x'\over p_3-x'}:
{x'\over p_4-x'}
\right]
$$
$$=\left[
{p_1\over x}:
{p_2\over x};
{p_3\over x'}:
{p_4\over x'}
\right]
=\left[
{p_1}:
{p_2};
{z p_3}:
{z p_4}
\right]
={z p_4-p_1\over p_2-p_1}\cdot {p_2-z p_3\over z p_4-z p_3}.
$$
As in Example~\ref{sDGASG}, we view the map ${\bold\Lambda}$ as the map from the compactified Jacobian:
$$\overline{\operatorname{Pic}^{1,1}C}=\Bbb P^1_z/_{\{ 0=\infty\}}\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1} \Bbb P^1_\lambda=\overline{M}_{0,4}.$$
The difference from Example~\ref{sDGASG} is that 
${\bold\Lambda}(0)={\bold\Lambda}(\infty)=\infty$. In particular, the scattering amplitude form, which  
is the $\operatorname{Pic}^{0,0} C$- invariant form $dz\over z$ 
written as a multivalued form of ~$\lambda$, has a log pole at $\infty$.
Explicitly, from the above, we have
\begin{equation}\label{afbfhafhdf}
\lambda z(p_2-p_1)(p_4-p_3)=(zp_4-p_1)(p_2-zp_3).
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
Solving \eqref{afbfhafhdf} for $z$ using quadratic formula gives,
after  algebraic manipulations,
$${dz\over z}=(p_1-p_2)(p_4-p_3){d\lambda\over\sqrt{f_2(\lambda)}},$$
where $f_2(\lambda)$ is the discriminant of this quadratic equation.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{aEFaf}
Suppose $(C, \vec d)$ is an MHV curve with a two-channel factorization into  components $A$ and $B$ by nodes $x$ and $y$. 
Let $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C$ be a generic line bundle. Then 
\begin{enumerate}
\item $d_A=g_A+1$, $d_B=g_B+1$.
\item $n_A=g_A+3$, $n_B=g_B+1$ (Case I) or $n_A=g_A+2$, $n_B=g_B+2$ (Case II).
\item $H^0(A,L_A)=H^0(B,L_B)=2$, \quad $H^1(A,L_A)=H^1(B,L_B)=0$.
\item $L_A$ and $L_B$ are globally generated.  
\item $\varphi_L(x)\ne\varphi_L(y)$. In particular, neither $A$ nor $B$ contains 
a degree $0$ rational component $C^{(0)}_i$ with both $x$ and $y$ as in Lemma~\ref{jhgkjgfk}.
\end{enumerate}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{adgbarhR}
The proof of the lemma shows how to build the map $\varphi_L:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$.
We~start with the maps $\varphi_{L_A}:\,A\to\Bbb P^1$ and $\varphi_{L_B}:\,B\to\Bbb P^1$ that each map $x$ and $y$ to different points.
Without loss of generality, we  can assume that 
$$\varphi_{L_A}(x)=\varphi_{L_B}(x)=0\quad\hbox{\rm and}\quad \varphi_{L_A}(y)=\varphi_{L_B}(y)=\infty.$$
The~maps $\varphi_{L_A}$ and $\varphi_{L_B}$ then glue and give a map $\varphi:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$, which corresponds 
to {\em some} line bundle $L$ that restricts to $L_A$ and $L_B$. 
The restriction map $$\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C\to  \operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d_A}A\times \operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d_B}B$$
has kernel $\Bbb C^*$. Changing $L$ by an element $z\in \Bbb C^*$
gives a map $\varphi_{zL}$ such that 
$$\varphi_{zL}|_A=\varphi_{L}|_A\quad\hbox{\rm and}\quad \varphi_{zL}|_B=z\varphi_{L}|_B.$$

\label{adgbarhR}
The proof of the lemma shows how to build the map $\varphi_L:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$.
We~start with the maps $\varphi_{L_A}:\,A\to\Bbb P^1$ and $\varphi_{L_B}:\,B\to\Bbb P^1$ that each map $x$ and $y$ to different points.
Without loss of generality, we  can assume that 
$$\varphi_{L_A}(x)=\varphi_{L_B}(x)=0\quad\hbox{\rm and}\quad \varphi_{L_A}(y)=\varphi_{L_B}(y)=\infty.$$
The~maps $\varphi_{L_A}$ and $\varphi_{L_B}$ then glue and give a map $\varphi:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$, which corresponds 
to {\em some} line bundle $L$ that restricts to $L_A$ and $L_B$. 
The restriction map $$\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C\to  \operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d_A}A\times \operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d_B}B$$
has kernel $\Bbb C^*$. Changing $L$ by an element $z\in \Bbb C^*$
gives a map $\varphi_{zL}$ such that 
$$\varphi_{zL}|_A=\varphi_{L}|_A\quad\hbox{\rm and}\quad \varphi_{zL}|_B=z\varphi_{L}|_B.$$

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{Review}\label{qfvefb} Curves $A$ and $B$ do not have to be stable. Suppose that $A$ is not stable\break (analysis for $B$ is the same). Then $A$ contains a rational component that in $C$ is attached to $x$ or $y$ (or both) which has less than $3$ special points (marked points or nodes) once $x$ and $y$ are separated. Since $C$ doesn't have a $1$-channel factorization, $A$ can't have a rational component with $1$ special point (in $A$) attached to both $x$ and~$y$. Thus $A$ is at least semi-stable. We list the remaining possibilities in Figure~\ref{fwgwrg}: \begin{figure}[htbp] \includegraphics[width=4.5in]{PicG.pdf} \caption{}\label{fwgwrg} \end{figure} \end{Review}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{safbsfhsfn}
A curve $C$ with a two-channel factorization such that $n_A=g_A+3$ and $n_B=g_B+1$
is an MHV curve if and only if 
\begin{enumerate}
\item The stabilization $\tilde A$ of $A$ with its $n_A$ marked points is an MHV  curve that does not contain 
a degree $0$ rational component $C^{(0)}_i$ as in \ref{asrsrh} with both $x$ and $y$.
\item The curve $\tilde B$ obtained by adding to $B$ two extra marked points in addition to its $n_B$ marked points
(at the nodes separating $A$ from $B$) is an MHV curve.
\end{enumerate}
A (multivalued) inverse of the map ${\bold\Lambda}$ at 
a general point $(p_1,\ldots,p_n)\in M_{0,n}$ can be found as follows:
Take $L_A\in{\bold\Lambda}_{A}^{-1}\left((p_i)_{i\in N_A}\right)$. 
Then $p'=\varphi_{L_A}(x)\ne \varphi_{L_A}(y)=p''$.
Take~$L_B\in{\bold\Lambda}_B^{-1}\left(p', (p_i)_{i\in N_B}, p''\right)$.
Finally, we can find $L$ using Amplification~\ref{adgbarhR}.

translation_statement_schema.claim: \label{safbsfhsfn} A curve $C$ with a two-channel factorization such that $n_A=g_A+3$ and $n_B=g_B+1$ is an MHV curve if and only if \begin{enumerate} \item The stabilization $\tilde A$ of $A$ with its $n_A$ marked points is an MHV curve that does not contain a degree $0$ rational component $C^{(0)}_i$ as in \ref{asrsrh} with both $x$ and $y$. \item The curve $\tilde B$ obtained by adding to $B$ two extra marked points in addition to its $n_B$ marked points (at the nodes separating $A$ from $B$)

In this section we will globalize the scattering amplitude map 
by the diagram
$$\begin{CD}
\operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}_0\Cal C        @>{\bold\Lambda}>>         M_{0,n}\\
@VVV                                          \\     
\overline{\Cal M}^{\operatorname{MHV}}_{g,n}
\end{CD}$$
where 
$\overline{\Cal M}^{\operatorname{MHV}}_{g,n}\subset\overline{\Cal M}_{g,n}$ is an open
locus of MHV curves,
$\operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}\Cal C$ is a universal MHV Picard family over it,
$\operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}_0\Cal C\subset\operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}\Cal C$ is an open locus and 
${\bold\Lambda}$
is 
the universal
scattering amplitude map. We also  compactify
$\operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}\Cal C$ by 
a~projective family $\overline{\cPic}^{MHV}\Cal C$ of compactified Jacobians.
Recall that $n=g+3$ and $d=g+1$.

theorem review_54 :
  ∃ (Q : Type*), Q = QuasiMapStack ∧
  ∃ (nu : Q → ModuliStack), nu = StabilizationMap ∧
  ∃ (substacks : Set Q), substacks.Nonempty ∧ IsOpen substacks ∧
  (∀ x ∈ substacks, ∃ U : Set Q, IsOpen U ∧ x ∈ U ∧ U ⊆ substacks) ∧
  (∀ x ∈ substacks, ∃ U : Set Q, IsOpen U ∧ x ∈ U ∧ U ⊆ substacks)

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
Consider the stack $\Cal Q_{g,n,d}$ of {\em quasi-maps} \cite{QM} of $C$ to $\Bbb P^1$.
Its sections over a scheme~$S$ are triples 
$(\Cal C,\Cal L,\alpha)$, where $\Cal C\to S$ is a family of semi-stable curves of genus $g$ with $n$ marked points,
$\Cal L$ is a line bundle on $\Cal C$ of relative degree $d$ and $\alpha:\,\Cal O_\Cal C^{\oplus 2}\to\Cal L$
is a homomorphism not vanishing on each fiber. The morphism  
$$\nu:\,\Cal Q_{g,n,d}\to\overline{\Cal M}_{g,n}$$
sends $\Cal C$ to its stabilization. The stack $\Cal Q_{g,n,d}$ (and its higher rank analogues) contains
many useful open proper separated substacks.

$\ $
\begin{enumerate}
\item 
$\overline{\Cal M}^{\operatorname{MHV}}_{g,n}$ is an open substack of 
$\overline{\Cal M}_{g,n}$.
\item 
$\Cal Q_{g,n,d}^{\operatorname{MHV}}$ is an open substack of the proper and separated Deligne--Mumford stack
$\overline{Q}_{g,n,d}$ of stable quotients as defined in \cite{MOP}.
\item The morphism $\nu:\,\Cal Q_{g,n,d}^{\operatorname{MHV}}\to\overline{\Cal M}^{\operatorname{MHV}}_{g,n}$ is smooth, of relative dimension $n$.
\item Consider the action of $\operatorname{GL}_2$ 
 on $\Cal Q_{g,n,d}^{\operatorname{MHV}}$ via its action on $\Cal O_\Cal C^{\oplus 2}$.
 The stabilizer of every point is the diagonal torus $\Bbb C^*$.
The quotient stack $\operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}_0\Cal C:=[\Cal Q_{g,n,d}^{\operatorname{MHV}}/PGL_2]  $ is 
an open substack in the rigidified Picard stack of pairs $(C,L)$, where $L$ is a degree $d$ line bundle on~$C$.
\item Let $\Delta_{ij} \subset(\Bbb P^1)^n$  be diagonals ($1\le i<j\le n$). We have a commutative diagram
\smallskip
$$\begin{CD}
\Cal Q_{g,n,d}^{\operatorname{MHV}} @>>>  (\Bbb P^1)^n\setminus\bigcup_{i,j}\Delta_{ij} \\
@VV{/PGL_2}V                                                     @VV{/{PGL_2}}V\\
\operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}_0\Cal C        @>{\bold\Lambda}>>         M_{0,n}\\
@VVV                                          \\     
\overline{\Cal M}^{\operatorname{MHV}}_{g,n}
\end{CD}$$
\end{enumerate}

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
\label{wgearhstjsrjrj}
$\ $
\begin{enumerate}
\item 
$\overline{\Cal M}^{\operatorname{MHV}}_{g,n}$ is an open substack of 
$\overline{\Cal M}_{g,n}$.
\item 
$\Cal Q_{g,n,d}^{\operatorname{MHV}}$ is an open substack of the proper and separated Deligne--Mumford stack
$\overline{Q}_{g,n,d}$ of stable quotients as defined in \cite{MOP}.
\item The morphism $\nu:\,\Cal Q_{g,n,d}^{\operatorname{MHV}}\to\overline{\Cal M}^{\operatorname{MHV}}_{g,n}$ is smooth, of relative dimension $n$.
\item Consider the action of $\operatorname{GL}_2$ 
 on $\Cal Q_{g,n,d}^{\operatorname{MHV}}$ via its action on $\Cal O_\Cal C^{\oplus 2}$.
 The stabilizer of every point is the diagonal torus $\Bbb C^*$.
The quotient stack $\operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}_0\Cal C:=[\Cal Q_{g,n,d}^{\operatorname{MHV}}/PGL_2]  $ is 
an open substack in the rigidified Picard stack of pairs $(C,L)$, where $L$ is a degree $d$ line bundle on~$C$.
\item Let $\Delta_{ij} \subset(\Bbb P^1)^n$  be diagonals ($1\le i<j\le n$). We have a commutative diagram
\smallskip
$$\begin{CD}
\Cal Q_{g,n,d}^{\operatorname{MHV}} @>>>  (\Bbb P^1)^n\setminus\bigcup_{i,j}\Delta_{ij} \\
@VV{/PGL_2}V                                                     @VV{/{PGL_2}}V\\
\operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}_0\Cal C        @>{\bold\Lambda}>>         M_{0,n}\\
@VVV                                          \\     
\overline{\Cal M}^{\operatorname{MHV}}_{g,n}
\end{CD}$$
\end{enumerate}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
Next we show that $\operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}_0\Cal C$ is separated (note that 
the quotient by a free action of an algebraic group is often not separated).
We will introduce a natural polarization $A$  on MHV curves and study slope-stability of line bundles $L$
with respect to it. It is well-known that 
slope stability on reducible curves
boils down to  {\em Gieseker's basic inequality}:
for any proper subcurve $Y\subset C$, we need to show
\begin{equation}\label{Gieseker_Ineq}
\left|d_Y-a_Y{d_C\over a_C}\right|<{1\over2}\#Y,
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
where 
$$d_Y=\deg L|_Y,\quad a_Y=\deg A|_Y\quad\hbox{\rm and}\quad 
\#Y:=|Y\cap\overline{ X\setminus Y}|.$$
Classically, the most studied case was $d_C=g-1$, $A=\omega_C$, and no marked points.
This is a convenient choice because ${d_C\over a_C}={1\over 2}$.
In the MHV Brill-Noether theory, when $d_C=g+1$ and marked points are present,
an equally convenient choice is a fractional polarization 
$$A=\omega_C+{4\over n}(p_1+\ldots+p_n),$$
which also gives ${d_C\over a_C}={1\over 2}$.
The $\Bbb Q$-line bundle $A$ is ample as long as  $C$ is stable curve without rational tails
(subcurves of arithmetic genus $0$ attached to the rest of the curve at one point),
which doesn't happen for  MHV curves by Lemma~\ref{qwefv2e2}.

entries.lean_statement:
theorem dwcsv {C : Type*} [AddCommGroup C] [Module ℝ C] [TopologicalSpace C] [T2Space C]
    (d_Y g_Y n_Y n : ℝ) (hC : Nonempty C) :
    (∀ (Y : Set C), Y.Nonempty → Y ≠ Set.univ → d_Y > g_Y - 1 + (2 / n) * n_Y) ↔
    (∀ (Y : Set C), Y.Nonempty → Y ≠ Set.univ → d_Y ≥ g_Y - 1 + (2 / n) * n_Y)

semantic_equivalence_artifact.normalized_natural_language_theorem: Quantifiers: for every Conclusion: A line bundle is A-stable if and only if d_Y > g_Y - 1 + (2/n) n_Y for every proper connected subcurve Y ⊂ C, where g_Y is the arithmetic genus of Y and n_Y is the number of marked points on it. For semi-stability, the inequality is not strict.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{Lemma} A line bundle is $A$-stable if and only if \begin{equation}\label{dwcsv} d_Y>g_Y-1+{2\over n}n_Y, \tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation} for every proper connected subcurve $Y\subset C$, where $g_Y$ is the arithmetic genus of $Y$ and $n_Y$ is the number of marked points on~it. For semi-stability, the inequality is not strict . \end{Lemma}

translation_statement_schema.claim: A line bundle is A-stable if and only if d_Y > g_Y - 1 + (2/n) n_Y for every proper connected subcurve Y ⊂ C, where g_Y is the arithmetic genus of Y and n_Y is the number of marked points on it. For semi-stability, the inequality is not strict.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
The family $\operatorname{\Cal P\mathit{ic}}^{MHV}_0\Cal C$ over $\overline{\Cal M}^{\operatorname{MHV}}_{g,n}$ is compactified by a projective 
(and hence separated) family
$\overline{\cPic}^{MHV}\Cal C$ of compactified Jacobians.  
Its geometric points represent $\operatorname{gr}$-equivalence
classes of $A$-semistable admissible sheaves on an MHV curve $C$.

entries.lean_statement: theorem amplification_61 (C : Type*) [AddCommGroup C] [Module ℝ C] [TopologicalSpace C] [T2Space C] (Γ : Type*) (d : ℕ) (d_i e_i l_i : Γ → ℕ) (theta_i : Γ → ℚ) (C_0 C_1 : Type*) (d_dt : C_1 → C_0) (H_1 : Type*) (theta : C_0 → ℚ) (x_i : C_1 → ℚ) (g n : ℕ) : ∀ (i : Γ), theta_i i = (-d_i i : ℚ) + (4 * l_i i : ℚ) / (d * n : ℚ) + (n * (e_i i - 2) : ℚ) / (2 * d : ℚ) → ∃ (polytopes : Finset (Finset ℚ)), polytopes.card = g ∧ (∀ p ∈ polytopes, p.card = g) ∧ (∀ p ∈ polytopes, ∀ q ∈ polytopes, p ≠ q → ∃ (gluing : ℚ), gluing ∈ p ∧ gluing ∈ q)

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
If all irreducible components of $C$ are rational then 
$\overline{\cPic}^{MHV}\Cal C$ is a stable toric variety of an algebraic torus $\operatorname{Pic}^0C\simeq(\Bbb C^*)^g$
and can be described as follows.
Choose one on-shell diagram for $C$, i.e.~endow each vertex $i$ of the dual graph
$\Gamma$  of $C$ with degree $d_i$ such that $\sum d_i=d$. 
For every vertex $i\in\Gamma$, 
\begin{enumerate}
\item 
$e_i$ is the number of edges (count each loop twice);
\item 
$l_i$ is the number of legs, i.e.~
the number of marked points on the corresponding irreducible 
component of~$C$;
\item
$\theta_i$ is the Oda--Seshadri number \cite{Oda}, in our case 
$$\theta_i=-d_i+{4l_i\over dn}+{n(e_i-2)\over 2d}.$$
\end{enumerate}
Let $C_0$ and $C_1$ be spaces of chains of the graph $\Gamma$ (with arbitrary coefficients).
Let $$\partial:\,C_1\to C_0$$ be the differential and let $H_1$ be the first homology group. 
We can view $\theta=(\theta_i)$ as a vector in $C_0(\Gamma,\Bbb Q)$. 
Consider the tiling of the affine space $\partial^{-1}(\theta)\subset C_1(\Gamma,\Bbb Q)$ by the hyperplanes $x_i={1\over2}+\Bbb Z$, where $x_i$
are the natural coordinates on $C_1$ (one coordinate for each edge of the graph). 
This affine space is parallel to the homology group $H_1(\Gamma,\Bbb Q)$ and the tiling has to be viewed modulo the action of $H_1(\Gamma,\Bbb Z)$.
Irreducible components
of the compactified Jacobian are given by the
$g$-dimensional polytopes of the tiling and the rest of the tiling determines how they are glued.

assumptions: Choose one on-shell diagram for $C$, i.e.~endow each vertex $i$ of the dual graph $\Gamma$ of $C$ with degree $d_i$ such that $\sum d_i=d$.
For every vertex $i\in\Gamma$, \begin{enumerate} \item $e_i$ is the number of edges (count each loop twice);

\item $\theta_i$ is the Oda--Seshadri number \cite{Oda}, in our case $$\theta_i=-d_i+{4l_i\over dn}+{n(e_i-2)\over 2d}.$$ \end{enumerate} Let $C_0$ and $C_1$ be spaces of chains of the graph $\Gamma$ (with arbitrary coefficients).
Let $$\partial:\,C_1\to C_0$$ be the differential and let $H_1$ be the first homology group.

Consider the tiling of the affine space $\partial^{-1}(\theta)\subset C_1(\Gamma,\Bbb Q)$ by the hyperplanes $x_i={1\over2}+\Bbb Z$, where $x_i$ are the natural coordinates on $C_1$ (one coordinate for each edge of the graph).
Irreducible components of the compactified Jacobian are given by the $g$-dimensional polytopes of the tiling and the rest of the tiling determines how they are glued.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{sdfvwfv}
Let 
$C=C_1\cup\ldots\cup C_4$
be the following curve of arithmetic genus~$1$: the wheel of four $\Bbb P^1$'s with marked points $p_i\in C_i$ for $i=1,\ldots,4$.
The~Picard group has two MHV components:
$$\operatorname{Pic}^{MHV}C=\operatorname{Pic}^{1,0,1,0}C\bigcup \operatorname{Pic}^{0,1,0,1}C.$$
Consider the first component. 
Since $L$ has degree $0$ on components $C_2$ and $C_4$,
the map $\varphi_L:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$ contracts  them to points, say $\varphi_L(C_2)=0$
and 
$\varphi_L(C_4)=\infty$. The restriction of $\varphi_L$ to $C_1$ and $C_3$ is an isomorphism. Thus $\varphi_L$ is completely determined by points $x=\varphi_L(p_1)$
and $y=\varphi_L(p_3)$. We have an identification
$$\operatorname{Pic}^{1,0,1,0}C\simeq\Bbb C^*,\quad z=y/x.$$ 
The scattering amplitude map
${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^{1,0,1,0}C\to\overline{M}_{0,4}\simeq\Bbb P^1$ is given by 
the cross-ratio of points $\varphi_L(p_1),\ldots,\varphi_L(p_4)$ varying with $L$.
This map is an isomorphism:
$$\lambda=[x:0;y:\infty]=
{\infty-x\over 0-x}\cdot {0-y\over \infty-y}=z.
$$
The scattering amplitude form is 
${dz\over z}={d\lambda\over\lambda}$
with log poles at $0$ and $\infty$. 
Next we compute the compactified Jacobian. There are $2$ types of stable line bundles
and $4$ types of semi-stable line bundles (in two $\operatorname{gr}$-equivalence classes),
see Figure~\ref{adfbadfh}.
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{semistable.pdf}
\caption{MHV compactified Jacobian in genus $1$}
\label{adfbadfh}
\end{figure}
Stable components are $\Bbb P^1$'s and $\operatorname{gr}$-equivalence classes of semi-stable line bundles correspond to their intersection 
points in the compactified Jacobian
$$\overline{\Pic}^{MHV}C=\overline{\Pic}^{1,0,1,0}C\bigcup \overline{\Pic}^{0,1,0,1}C=\Bbb P^1\cup\Bbb P^1.$$
One can visualize topology on $\overline{\Pic}^{MHV}C$ through ``chip-firing'' of black chips.

$p\mapsto \tau(p)$ (whenever it exists, the hyperelliptic involution is unique).

associated with the line bundle

of the hyperelliptic involution).

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
Let $\operatorname{Bun}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$ be the smooth algebraic stack of 
{\em quasi-parabolic vector bundles} $F$ on $\Bbb P^1$ of rank $2$ with trivial determinant \cite{Pauly}.
Recall that a quasi-parabolic structure on a vector bundle is  a choice of a line $V_i\subset F|_{z_i}$ over each marked point. This data determines a ruled surface $\Bbb P(F)\to\Bbb P^1$ of even degree and points
$$q_i=\Bbb P(V_i)\subset\Bbb P(F|_{z_i})\quad\hbox{\rm for $i=1,\ldots,n$}.$$

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
A generic parabolic bundle $F$ is a trivial bundle $\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb C^2$ with lines $\{z_i\}\times V_i$ for  $V_i\subset\Bbb C^2$\footnote{Indeed, the trivial vector bundle on $\Bbb P^1$ does not deform, therefore
these parabolic bundles are parametrized by a non-empty open substack of $\operatorname{Bun}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$. On the other hand,
all parabolic bundles of rank $2$ and degree $0$ can be deformed to a parabolic bundle with trivial $F$.} .
The corresponding ruled surface is the product $\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb P^1$ with points $(z_1,q_1),\ldots,(z_n,q_n)\in\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb P^1$,
where $q_i=\Bbb P(V_i)$.
Thus we have a birational map $\Xi:\,\operatorname{Bun}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)\dashrightarrow M_{0,n}$
defined as in Figure~\ref{shasrhar}.
%$$\Xi:\,\operatorname{Bun}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)\dashrightarrow M_{0,n},\quad
%(F;V_1,\ldots,V_n)\mapsto (q_1,\ldots,q_n).$$

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
A generic parabolic bundle $F$ is a trivial bundle $\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb C^2$ with lines $\{z_i\}\times V_i$ for  $V_i\subset\Bbb C^2$\footnote{Indeed, the trivial vector bundle on $\Bbb P^1$ does not deform, therefore
these parabolic bundles are parametrized by a non-empty open substack of $\operatorname{Bun}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$. On the other hand,
all parabolic bundles of rank $2$ and degree $0$ can be deformed to a parabolic bundle with trivial $F$.} .
The corresponding ruled surface is the product $\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb P^1$ with points $(z_1,q_1),\ldots,(z_n,q_n)\in\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb P^1$,
where $q_i=\Bbb P(V_i)$.
Thus we have a birational map $\Xi:\,\operatorname{Bun}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)\dashrightarrow M_{0,n}$
defined as in Figure~\ref{shasrhar}.
%$$\Xi:\,\operatorname{Bun}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)\dashrightarrow M_{0,n},\quad
%(F;V_1,\ldots,V_n)\mapsto (q_1,\ldots,q_n).$$

entries.lean_statement:
theorem SSRHS (C : Type) [HyperellipticCurve C] (g : ℕ) (z : Fin n → ℂ)
    (L : Pic^{g+1} C) (h : ℕ) (K : ℕ) (tau : ℂ → ℂ) (x : Fin (g-1) → C) (p : Fin n → C)
    (hK : K = (g-1)*h) (htau : ∀ i, tau (x i) = x i) (hp : ∀ i, p i ≠ tau (p i))
    (hL : L ≠ 0) (hE : E ≠ ∅) :
    ∃ (Λ : Pic^{g+1} C → Bun (ℙ1) (z : Fin n → ℂ)) (Ξ : Bun (ℙ1) (z : Fin n → ℂ) → M_{0,n}),
    (Λ L).splittingType = (𝒪 ⊕ 𝒪) ∧
    (∀ i, (Λ L).parabolicLines i = [s1 (p i), s2 (p i)]) ∧
    Λ L = (phi_h)_* L ∧
    Ξ (Λ L) = ([s1 (p 0) : s2 (p 0)], ..., [s1 (p (n-1)) : s2 (p (n-1))])

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{SSRHS}
The scattering amplitude map of a hyperelliptic curve $C$ is a composition 
$${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C\mathop{\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits}^{\overline{\bold\Lambda}} \operatorname{Bun}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)\mathop{\dashrightarrow}^\Xi M_{0,n}.$$
The bundle $\overline{\bold\Lambda}(L)$ has splitting type $\Cal O\oplus\Cal O$ away from the theta-divisor $E$ of Corollary~\ref{zxbzfn}.

entries.lean_statement: theorem mb_hjb_hb (n : ℕ) (alpha : Fin n → ℝ) (F : Type*) [AddCommGroup F] [Module ℝ F] [QuasiParabolicBundle F n] (halpha : ∀ i, 0 < alpha i ∧ alpha i ≤ 1 / 2) : ∀ (L : Type*) [AddCommGroup L] [Module ℝ L] [Submodule L F], (∀ i, L ≤ F) → (Module.rank ℝ L = 1 → (Module.rank ℝ F = 2 → (∀ I : Finset (Fin n), (∑ i in I, alpha i) + Module.rank ℝ L < (1 / 2) * (∑ i : Fin n, alpha i))))

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[stability]
In view of Lemma~\ref{SSRHS}, in the hyperelliptic case the scattering amplitude map
combines effects of the birational 
morphism $\Xi$, which as we will see only depends on the point $o=(z_1,\ldots,z_n)\in M_{0,n}$
and the map~$\overline{\bold\Lambda}$, which is a finer invariant in the hyperelliptic case.
Moreover,  the divisor $E$ of $\operatorname{Pic}^dC$ is mapped to a divisor by $\overline{\bold\Lambda}$ but is collapsed 
to the point $o$ by ${\bold\Lambda}$,
which creates a singularity for the probability measure (see discussion of the real case in Section~\ref{sdcqwv}).
However, at the moment the target of $\overline{\bold\Lambda}$ is the stack of quasi-parabolic bundles.
To make $\overline{\bold\Lambda}$ more concrete, we will choose a projective model for the stack
(at the price of making $\overline{\bold\Lambda}$ a rational~map).
There is a notion of slope-stability that depends on a choice of a parabolic weight.
Concretely, the weight $\vec\alpha$ is a sequence $\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_n$ of real numbers with $0<\alpha_i\le {1\over 2}$.
A quasi-parabolic bundle $(F;V_1,\ldots,V_n)$ is $\vec\alpha$-stable (resp.~semistable) parabolic bundle
if and only if every line sub-bundle $L\subset F$ satisfies the  {\em slope inequality}
\begin{equation}\label{mb,hjb,hb}
k+\sum_{i\in I}\alpha_i<{1\over 2}\sum\limits_{i=1}^n\alpha_i\quad (\hbox{\rm resp.}\quad \le),
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
where $k=\deg L$ and $I\subset\{1,\ldots,n\}$ is a subset of indices such that $L|_{z_i}=V_i$.
We denote the corresponding moduli space by $\operatorname{Bun}_{\vec\alpha}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$ or simply
by  $\operatorname{Bun}_\alpha(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$ if $\alpha_1=\ldots=\alpha_n=\alpha$.
Here is a summary of  wall-crossing with chambers given by inequalities \eqref{mb,hjb,hb}.

We denote the corresponding moduli space by $\operatorname{Bun}_{\vec\alpha}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$ or simply by $\operatorname{Bun}_\alpha(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$ if $\alpha_1=\ldots=\alpha_n=\alpha$.
