semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
First we consider the {\em standard case}
$\sum\limits_{i=1}^n\alpha_i>2$ as in 
\cite{Bauer, Mukai,Casagrande,Kumar,Araujo}. 
If $\vec\alpha$ is strictly semistable
then
$$\operatorname{Pic}\operatorname{Bun}_{\vec\alpha}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)=\Bbb Z^{n+1}.$$
The Fano model (i.e.~ the model with $-K$ ample) is 
\begin{equation}\label{asdvqfv}
\operatorname{Bun}_{1\over2}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n),
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
which is a smooth variety if $n$ is odd 
%(this is the Fano variety of $n-5\over2$-planes in the  intersection of two quadrics in $\Bbb P^{n-1}$ \cite{Casagrande})
and has isolated singularities
if $n$ is even. 
As~$\alpha$ increases from $2\over n$ to $1\over 2$, $\operatorname{Bun}_{\alpha}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$
undergoes  the anticanonical minimal model program, i.e.~
the sequence of birational transformations that makes $-K$ more and more positive at every step. It proceeds as follows~\cite{Bauer}:
\begin{enumerate}
\item $\operatorname{Bun}_{2\over n}=\Bbb P^{n-3}$ with $(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$ embedded into $\Bbb P^{n-3}$ as a rational normal curve of degree $n-3$.
The MMP starts with
$\operatorname{Bun}_{{2\over n}+\varepsilon}=\operatorname{Bl}_{z_1,\ldots,z_n}\Bbb P^{n-3}$. 
\item The first birational transformation ``antiflips'' several $\Bbb P^1$'s, namely lines connecting points $z_1,\ldots,z_n$ pairwise and the rational normal curve.
Each of these $\Bbb P^1$'s is  blown-up and the exceptional divisor contracted onto~$\Bbb P^{n-5}$.
\item On the following steps, we antiflip certain $\Bbb P^k$'s analogously.
\item MMP stops when $-K$ becomes big and nef on $\operatorname{Bun}_{{1\over 2}-\varepsilon}$, in fact even ample when $n$~is odd.
If~$n$~is even, the anticanonical model
$\operatorname{Bun}_{1\over 2}$ is obtained by contracting certain $K$-trivial $\Bbb P^{n-4\over 2}$'s in $\operatorname{Bun}_{{1\over 2}-\varepsilon}$ to  
singular points.
\end{enumerate}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
The {\em special case} $\sum\limits_{i=1}^n\alpha_i<2$ was studied in \cite{Moon}, in which case
$$\operatorname{Pic}\operatorname{Bun}_{\vec\alpha}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)=\Bbb Z^n$$ 
if $\vec\alpha$ is strictly semistable.
The Fano model  is the symmetric GIT quotient 
$$(\Bbb P^1)^n//\operatorname{PGL}_2,$$
which is smooth if $n$ is odd and with isolated singularities
if $n$ is even. More generally, in the special case
$\operatorname{Bun}_{\vec\alpha}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$ is the GIT quotient $(\Bbb P^1)^n//_{\vec\alpha}\operatorname{PGL}_2$
with respect to fractional polarization $\vec\alpha$.
The map   
$$\Xi^{-1}:\,M_{0,n}\dashrightarrow \operatorname{Bun}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$$
is equivalent to the natural embedding 
$\Xi^{-1}:\,M_{0,n}\hookrightarrow (\Bbb P^1)^n//_{\vec\alpha}\operatorname{PGL}_2$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
The transition from the special (2)  to the standard (1) cases goes as follows.
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(2)] All stable parabolic bundles have splitting type $\Cal O\oplus\Cal O$. 
The product $\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb P^1$ with points $(z_1,z_1),\ldots,(z_n,z_n)$ is stable
giving the point $o\in M_{0,n}$. 
\item[(1)] The product $\Bbb P^1\times\Bbb P^1$ above is 
destabilized by $\Cal O(-1)$ embedded by the 
sequence $0\to \Cal O(-1)\to\Cal O\oplus\Cal O\to\Cal O(1)\to0$. The~point $o$ is blown-up.
The 
exceptional divisor parametrizes
parabolic bundles of   type $\Cal O(-1)\oplus\Cal O(1)$.
\end{enumerate}
We summarize the discussion by the following diagram:

    model1 = (BlowUp ℙ2 (Finset.univ.image (fun i => z i))) ∧

    model1 = dP4 ∧

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
Suppose $g=2$, $n=5$. Apart from $\Bbb P^2$ there are only two models:
$$\operatorname{Bun}_{{2\over5}+}=\operatorname{Bun}_{1\over2}\simeq\operatorname{Bl}_{z_1,\ldots,z_5}\Bbb P^2=\bold{dP}_4\qquad\hbox{\rm (standard)}$$
and 
$$\operatorname{Bun}_{{2\over5}-}\simeq\operatorname{Bl}_4\Bbb P^2=\bold{dP}_5\qquad\hbox{\rm (special),}$$
the quartic and quintic del Pezzo surfaces.
The morphism 
$$\Xi:\,\bold{dP}_4\to\bold{dP}_5\simeq\overline{M}_{0,5}$$ 
contracts the conic through $z_1,\ldots,z_5$ to the point $o=(z_1,\ldots,z_5)\in M_{0,5}$.
This morphism can also be described as projection of 
$z_1,\ldots,z_5\in\Bbb P^2$ to $\Bbb P^1$ from a varying point of $\Bbb P^2$ (cf.~\cite{CT_Cont}).
We will further study this example in Section~\ref{sfgasgsRH}.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{Theorem}\label{wefvev} Consider the induced map $\overline{\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C\dashrightarrow \operatorname{Bun}_{1\over2}(\Bbb P^1;z_1,\ldots,z_n)$. \begin{enumerate} \item $\overline{\bold\Lambda}$ is regular away from loci $U(k,I)$ (of codimension at least~$2$) of line bundles $$\Cal O(kh+\sum_{i\in I} p_i+x_1+\ldots+x_j)\subset\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C\quad\hbox{\rm for}\quad x_1,\ldots,x_j\in C,$$ $$g+1=2k+|I|+j,\qquad j<{g-1\over 2}.$$ Loci $U(k,I)$ with equality $j = {g-1\over 2}$ give strictly semistable parabolic bundles. \item $\overline{\bold\Lambda}^*(-K)\equiv 4\Theta$. \end{enumerate} \end{Theorem}

entries.lean_statement: theorem sGSGsrg (f : Polynomial ℂ) (g : ℕ) (hf_deg : f.degree = 2 * g + 2) (hf_nmr : ∀ z, f.eval z = 0 → f.derivative.eval z ≠ 0) (U V W : Polynomial ℂ) (hU : U.degree ≤ g + 1) (hV : V.degree ≤ g + 1) (hW : W.degree ≤ g + 1) : ∃ V' : Polynomial ℂ, V' = -V ∧ V^2 + U * W = f

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
The curve $C$ has equation $y^2=f(z)$, where $f\in\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}(2g+2)$ is a polynomial without multiple roots.
The map $\pi:=\varphi_h:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$ is the projection $(z,y)\mapsto z$.
The points $(z_0, 0)$ with $z_0$ a root of $f(z)$ are the Weierstrass points.
As the sheaf of $\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}$-modules, we have 
$$\pi_*\Cal O_C=\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}(-g-1)\oplus\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}.$$
The sheaf of algebras structure of $\pi_*\Cal O_C$ is completely determined  by the
map $\operatorname{Sym}^2\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}(-g-1)\to\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}$, which is simply
multiplication by $f\in\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}(2g+2)$.
By Lemma~\ref{SSRHS},
when $L \in \operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}(C)$ is away from the theta-divisor~$E$, we have
$$\pi_* L=\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}\oplus\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}.$$
The action of $\pi_*\Cal O_C$ on $\pi_* L$ is determined by a $2\times 2$ matrix 
\begin{equation}\label{sGSGsrg}
M=\left[\begin{matrix} V&U\cr W& V'\cr\end{matrix}\right]\in \operatorname{Hom}(\mathcalO_{\Bbb P^1}(-g-1)\otimes 
(\mathcalO_{\Bbb P^1}\oplus\mathcalO_{\Bbb P^1}), \mathcalO_{\Bbb P^1}\oplus\mathcalO_{\Bbb P^1}).
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
of polynomials $U,V,V',W$ in $z$ of degree at most $g+1$. Applying $M$ twice should be a multiplication by the polynomial $f(z)$, which gives
equations on polynomials
$$V'=-V$$
and
\begin{equation}\label{dfbzdfhz}
-\det(M)=V^2+UW=f(z).
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
Let $S(f)\subset \Bbb A^{3(g+2)}$ be the affine subvariety given by equations \eqref{dfbzdfhz},
where $\Bbb A^{3(g+2)}$ parametrizes $U,V,W$.
Then $S(f)$ is smooth  for any  
polynomial $f(z)$ without multiple roots. Moreover, the group
$\operatorname{PGL}_2$ acts on $S(f)$ (with elements viewed as $2\times2$ matrices) by conjugation freely and the quotient 
is, by \cite[Theoreme 1.4]{Beauville},
\begin{equation}\label{SFhDFh}
S(f)/\operatorname{PGL}_2\simeq \operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C\setminus E.
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}

entries.lean_statement: theorem sfgqwrgqwrg {n : ℕ} (p : Fin n → ℝ) (z : Fin n → ℝ) (q : Fin n → ℝ) (f : ℝ → ℝ) (h_not_weierstrass : ∀ i, ¬ IsWeierstrassPoint (p i)) (h_M_distinct_eigenspaces : ∀ i, (M (z i)).eigenspaces.card = 2) (h_slopes_fixed : ∀ i, q i ∈ EigenspaceSlopes (M (z i))) : Nat.card {s : ℝ | ∀ i, (M (z i)).eigenspaceWithSlope (q i) ≠ ⊥} / 2 ^ n = scattering_amplitude_degree_2g f

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{sfgqwrgqwrg}
Suppose  that neither of the points $p_1,\ldots,p_n$ is a Weierstrass point.
Then $(\pi_*L)|_{z_i}=L|_{p_i}\oplus L|_{\tau(p_i)}$ and thus the $2\times 2$ matrix $M(z_i)$ has two distinct eigenspaces, one corresponds to the parabolic structure given by $p_i$
and another by $\tau(p_i)$. The point $q_i\in\Bbb P^1$ gives the slope of this eigenspace. 
Thus we need to do the following: 
\begin{enumerate}
\item Count the number of solutions
in $S(f)$ such that each matrix $M(z_i)$ has an eigenspace with a fixed general 
slope $q_i$.
\item Divide this number by $2^n$. Indeed, 
using $p_i$ or $\tau(p_i)$
gives the scattering amplitude of the same degree by Proposition~\ref{sadvfb}.
\end{enumerate}
Note that taking conjugacy classes of matrices by the $\operatorname{PGL}_2$ action is not necessary: 
fixing three  different slopes of eigenspaces eliminates the conjugacy action.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{srfqwrg}
The smooth solution set $S(f)\subset\Bbb C^{3g+6}$ of dimension $g+3$ 
is the intersection of $2g+3$ affine quadrics, one for each coefficient of the degree $2g+2$ polynomial~$f(z)$.
Solutions at infinity $\Bbb P^{3g+5}$ are given by the homogeneous equation $V^2=UW$, which has expected dimension $g+2$.
Indeed, if $V=0$ then either $U=0$ or $W=0$, which gives a union of two projective subspaces of dimension $g+1$ each.
If $V\ne0$ then the solution is determined by $V$ up to reordering of terms in the polynomial factorization and rescaling $U$ by $\lambda$
and $W$ by $\lambda^{-1}$.
Imposing the slope $q_i$ at $z_i$ is a linear equation on $U,V,W$. Since $\det M(z_i)\ne0$ but $\operatorname{Tr} M(z_i)=0$,
no matrix $M(z_i)$ has more than two eigenspaces, and so these linear equations have no base locus on $S(f)$.
By Bertini Theorem, the intersection is transversal and has expected dimension.
We claim that there are no solutions at infinity. Indeed, the solution set of the homogeneous system of equations
$V^2=UW$ can be thought of as $2\times 2$ matrices and is covered by $n$ open charts where $M(z_i)\ne0$.
Since $\det M(z_i)=\operatorname{Tr} M(z_i)=0$, $M(z_i)$ is a non-zero nilpotent matrix and thus have only one eigenspace.
Thus the base locus of the solution set is empty and so it has an expected dimension by Bertini Theorem, 
which means it is empty.

Indeed, the solution set of the homogeneous system of equations $V^2=UW$ can be thought of as $2\times 2$ matrices and is covered by $n$ open charts where $M(z_i)\ne0$.
translation_statement_schema.claim: Indeed, if $V=0$ then either $U=0$ or $W=0$, which gives a union of two projective subspaces of dimension $g+1$ each. If $V\ne0$ then the solution is determined by $V$ up to reordering of terms in the polynomial factorization and rescaling $U$ by $\lambda$ and $W$ by $\lambda^{-1}$. Imposing the slope $q_i$ at $z_i$ is a linear equation on $U,V,W$. semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
The space of matrices of polynomials with fixed slopes $q_i$  is a linear space of dimension $3(g+2)-n=2g+3$ and we are counting
intersection points of $2g+3$ quadrics under the assumption that intersection is transersal and does not run away to infinity.
Thus we have $2^{2g+3}$ intersection points
and therefore 
$$\deg\overline{\bold\Lambda}={2^{2g+3}\over 2^n}=2^g.$$

translation_statement_schema.claim: The space of matrices of polynomials with fixed slopes $q_i$ is a linear space of dimension $3(g+2)-n=2g+3$ and we are counting intersection points of $2g+3$ quadrics under the assumption that intersection is transersal and does not run away to infinity. Thus we have $2^{2g+3}$ intersection points and therefore $$\deg\overline{\bold\Lambda}={2^{2g+3}\over 2^n}=2^g.$$

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
If some of the points $z_i$ are Weierstrass points, i.e.~$p_i=\tau(p_i)$, the argument goes as before, except for two issues:
\begin{enumerate}
\item We don't have to divide the number of solutions by $2$ as in \ref{sfgqwrgqwrg} (2).
\item By Claim~\ref{wef	wef}, $M(z_i)$ is a non-zero nilpotent matrix. 
The subvariety of nilpotent $2\times 2$ matrices is a quadric cone
and fixing the slope $q_i$ gives a ruling of this cone (intersection with the tangent plane of multiplicity~$2$).
Thus fixing the slope is not equivalent to pulling back a general
hyperplane by a  morphism to projective space as in \ref{srfqwrg}. 
The correct application of Bertini Theorem is to project this cone onto a conic (isomorphic to $\Bbb P^1$)
and count this solution only once.
\end{enumerate}
Thus issues (1) and (2) cancel each other and we get the same count.

theorem asrgarh {C : Type*} [AddCommGroup C] [Module ℝ C] [TopologicalSpace C] [T2Space C]
  (f U V W : Polynomial ℝ) (g : ℕ) (c : ℝ) (c_1 c_g : Fin g → ℝ) (M : Set (Polynomial ℝ × Polynomial ℝ × Polynomial ℝ)) (A : ℝ) (A_vee : ℝ) (E : Set (ℝ × ℝ)) (Q : Set (ℝ × ℝ)) (M_0_n : Set (ℝ × ℝ)) (PGL_2 : Set (ℝ → ℝ)) (Pic_g_plus_1_C : Set (ℝ × ℝ)) (E : Set (ℝ × ℝ)) (h_f : IsMonic f) (h_deg_f : Polynomial.degree f = 2 * g + 1) (h_U : IsMonic U) (h_deg_U : Polynomial.degree U = g) (h_deg_V : Polynomial.degree V = g - 1) (h_W : IsMonic W) (h_deg_W : Polynomial.degree W = g + 1) (h_U_no_multiple_roots : ∀ i j, i ≠ j → (U.coeff i) ≠ (U.coeff j)) (h_V_interp : ∀ i, i ≤ g → V.eval (U.root i) = ±Real.sqrt (f.eval (U.root i))) (h_U_divides : ∀ z, U.eval z ∣ (f.eval z - (V.eval z)^2)) (h_W_def : ∀ z, W.eval z = (f.eval z - (V.eval z)^2) / (U.eval z)) (h_translation_invariant : ∀ c, ∃ dot_F, dot_F = (dot_U, dot_V, dot_W)) (h_dot_U_eq : ∀ c, dot_U = (V.eval c * U - U.eval c * V) / (z - c)) (h_dot_V_eq : ∀ c, dot_V = (1/2) * (U.eval c * W - W.eval c * U) / (z - c) - U.eval c * U) (h_dot_W_eq : ∀ c, dot_W = (W.eval c * V - V.eval c * W) / (z - c) + U.eval c * V) (h_independent_fields : LinearIndependent (Fin g) (fun i => dot_F_i)) (h_A_vee_def : A_vee = ∧ i, dot_F_i) (h_A_dual : A_vee = Dual A) (h_scattering_amplitude_map : M → M_0_n) (h_E_map : M → (P^1)^n) (h_Q_map : (P^1)^n → M_0_n) (h_Q_quotient : Q_map = QuotientBy PGL_2) (h_E_slopes : ∀ i, E_map i = (y_i - V.eval z_i) / U.eval z_i) : M ≅ Pic_g_plus_1_C \ E

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[\cite{Mumford}]\label{asrgarh}
In the model \eqref{SFhDFh}, one can eliminate 
the $\operatorname{PGL}_2$-action by making
\begin{itemize}
\item $f$ a monic polynomial of degree $2g+1$ (one of the roots is moved to $\infty$),
\item $U$ a monic polynomial of degree $g$,
\item $V$ a polynomial of degree $g-1$,
\item $W$ a monic polynomial of degree $g+1$.
\end{itemize}
Under these conditions, the solution set $M$ of equations \eqref{dfbzdfhz} in $\Bbb A^{3g+1}$
is isomorphic to $\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C\setminus E$. Solutions look as follows.
Suppose $U(z)=(z-t_1)\ldots(z-t_g)$ has no multiple roots. Choose $s_i=\pm\sqrt{f(t_i)}$ for $i=1,\ldots,g$
and use Lagrange interpolation to find $V(z)$ such that $V(t_i)=s_i$  for $i=1,\ldots,g$.
Then $U(z)$ divides $f(z)-V(z)^2$ and we can define $W(z)={\displaystyle f(z)-V(z)^2\over \displaystyle U(z)}$.


For any $c\in\Bbb P^1$, the Lax pair differential equation
gives a translation-invariant vector field $\dot F=(\dot U(z),\dot V(z),\dot W(z))$ on $M$ with components
$$\dot U(z)={V(c)U(z)-U(c)V(z)\over z-c};$$
$$\dot V(z)={1\over 2}{U(c)W(z)-W(c)U(z)\over z-c}-U(c)U(z);$$
$$\dot W(z)={W(c)V(z)-V(c)W(z)\over z-c}+U(c)V(z).$$
General points $c_1,\ldots,c_g$ gives linearly independent translation-invariant vector fields
$\dot F_1,\ldots,\dot F_g$ and thus a translation-invariant polyvector field
$A^\vee=\dot F_1\wedge\ldots\wedge\dot F_g$, which is  dual to the translation-invariant volume form $A$ on $\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$.
Applying $d{\bold\Lambda}$ to $A^\vee$ and dualizing gives the value of the branch of the scattering amplitude form 
that corresponds to the point $(U,V,W)$. More concretely, we can factor the scattering amplitude map
${\bold\Lambda}:\,M\to M_{0,n}$ into maps
$$E:\, M\dashrightarrow (\Bbb P^1)^n\quad\hbox{\rm and}\quad Q:\,(\Bbb P^1)^n\dashrightarrow M_{0,n},$$
where $Q$ is the quotient by the $\operatorname{PGL}_2$ action and $E$ is the map that assigns to a matrix of polynomials \eqref{sGSGsrg}
the slopes of its eigenspaces 
$$
{y_1-V(z_1)\over U(z_1)},\ldots, {y_n-V(z_n)\over U(z_n)}
$$
at marked points $p_i=(z_i,y_i)$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$.

For any $c\in\Bbb P^1$, the Lax pair differential equation gives a translation-invariant vector field $\dot F=(\dot U(z),\dot V(z),\dot W(z))$ on $M$ with components $$\dot U(z)={V(c)U(z)-U(c)V(z)\over z-c};$$ $$\dot V(z)={1\over 2}{U(c)W(z)-W(c)U(z)\over z-c}-U(c)U(z);$$ $$\dot W(z)={W(c)V(z)-V(c)W(z)\over z-c}+U(c)V(z).$$ General points $c_1,\ldots,c_g$ gives linearly independent translation-invariant vector fields $\dot F_1,\ldots,\dot F_g$ and thus a translation-invariant polyvector field $A^\vee=\dot F_1\wedge\ldots\wedge\dot F_g$, which is dual to the translation-invariant volume form $A$ on $\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$.

More concretely, we can factor the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}:\,M\to M_{0,n}$ into maps $$E:\, M\dashrightarrow (\Bbb P^1)^n\quad\hbox{\rm and}\quad Q:\,(\Bbb P^1)^n\dashrightarrow M_{0,n},$$ where $Q$ is the quotient by the $\operatorname{PGL}_2$ action and $E$ is the map that assigns to a matrix of polynomials \eqref{sGSGsrg} the slopes of its eigenspaces $$ {y_1-V(z_1)\over U(z_1)},\ldots, {y_n-V(z_n)\over U(z_n)} $$ at marked points $p_i=(z_i,y_i)$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$.

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
[\cite{Mumford}]\label{asrgarh}
In the model \eqref{SFhDFh}, one can eliminate 
the $\operatorname{PGL}_2$-action by making
\begin{itemize}
\item $f$ a monic polynomial of degree $2g+1$ (one of the roots is moved to $\infty$),
\item $U$ a monic polynomial of degree $g$,
\item $V$ a polynomial of degree $g-1$,
\item $W$ a monic polynomial of degree $g+1$.
\end{itemize}
Under these conditions, the solution set $M$ of equations \eqref{dfbzdfhz} in $\Bbb A^{3g+1}$
is isomorphic to $\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C\setminus E$. Solutions look as follows.
Suppose $U(z)=(z-t_1)\ldots(z-t_g)$ has no multiple roots. Choose $s_i=\pm\sqrt{f(t_i)}$ for $i=1,\ldots,g$
and use Lagrange interpolation to find $V(z)$ such that $V(t_i)=s_i$  for $i=1,\ldots,g$.
Then $U(z)$ divides $f(z)-V(z)^2$ and we can define $W(z)={\displaystyle f(z)-V(z)^2\over \displaystyle U(z)}$.


For any $c\in\Bbb P^1$, the Lax pair differential equation
gives a translation-invariant vector field $\dot F=(\dot U(z),\dot V(z),\dot W(z))$ on $M$ with components
$$\dot U(z)={V(c)U(z)-U(c)V(z)\over z-c};$$
$$\dot V(z)={1\over 2}{U(c)W(z)-W(c)U(z)\over z-c}-U(c)U(z);$$
$$\dot W(z)={W(c)V(z)-V(c)W(z)\over z-c}+U(c)V(z).$$
General points $c_1,\ldots,c_g$ gives linearly independent translation-invariant vector fields
$\dot F_1,\ldots,\dot F_g$ and thus a translation-invariant polyvector field
$A^\vee=\dot F_1\wedge\ldots\wedge\dot F_g$, which is  dual to the translation-invariant volume form $A$ on $\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$.
Applying $d{\bold\Lambda}$ to $A^\vee$ and dualizing gives the value of the branch of the scattering amplitude form 
that corresponds to the point $(U,V,W)$. More concretely, we can factor the scattering amplitude map
${\bold\Lambda}:\,M\to M_{0,n}$ into maps
$$E:\, M\dashrightarrow (\Bbb P^1)^n\quad\hbox{\rm and}\quad Q:\,(\Bbb P^1)^n\dashrightarrow M_{0,n},$$
where $Q$ is the quotient by the $\operatorname{PGL}_2$ action and $E$ is the map that assigns to a matrix of polynomials \eqref{sGSGsrg}
the slopes of its eigenspaces 
$$
{y_1-V(z_1)\over U(z_1)},\ldots, {y_n-V(z_n)\over U(z_n)}
$$
at marked points $p_i=(z_i,y_i)$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$.

theorem lemma_91 {C : Type*} (p : Fin 5 → C) (K : C) (E : C) (Ei : Fin 5 → C) (Eij : Fin 5 → Fin 5 → C) (delta : C) (deltai : Fin 5 → C) (deltaij : Fin 5 → Fin 5 → C) (hC : Genus2Curve C) (hAssumption : Assumption Sfbzfdhadfh) :
  (∀ i j, i ≠ j → ¬(HyperellipticInvolution C) (p i) = p j) ∧
  (∀ i j k, i ≠ j → j ≠ k → i ≠ k → ¬Collinear ℝ ({p i, p j, p k} : Set C)) ∧
  (E ≠ Ei 0 ∧ E ≠ Ei 1 ∧ E ≠ Ei 2 ∧ E ≠ Ei 3 ∧ E ≠ Ei 4 ∧
   ∀ i j, i ≠ j → Ei i ≠ Ei j ∧
   ∀ i j k l, i ≠ j → k ≠ l → Eij i j ≠ Eij k l) ∧
  (delta ≠ deltai 0 ∧ delta ≠ deltai 1 ∧ delta ≠ deltai 2 ∧ delta ≠ deltai 3 ∧ delta ≠ deltai 4 ∧
   ∀ i j, i ≠ j → deltai i ≠ deltai j ∧
   ∀ i j k l, i ≠ j → k ≠ l → deltaij i j ≠ deltaij k l) ∧
  ∀ i j, i ≠ j → delta ∉ {Eij i j}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
In genus~$2$, Assumption~\ref{Sfbzfdhadfh} is equivalent to any of the following:
\begin{enumerate}
\item No two points of $p_1,\ldots,p_5$ are related by the hyperelliptic involution.
\item No three points of $p_1,\ldots,p_5\in\Bbb P^3$ are collinear. 
\item $16$ divisors $E$, $E_i$, $E_{ij}$ are pairwise different.
\item $16$ points $\delta$, $\delta_i$, $\delta_{ij}$ are pairwise different.
%\item For any $i\ne j$, $\delta-p_i-p_j$ is not effective. 
\item $\delta\not\in E_{ij}$ for any $i\ne j$.
\end{enumerate}

translation_statement_schema.claim: Assumption Sfbzfdhadfh is equivalent to any of the following: (1) No two points of p_1, ..., p_5 are related by the hyperelliptic involution. (2) No three points of p_1, ..., p_5 in P^3 are collinear. (3) 16 divisors E, E_i, E_ij are pairwise different. (4) 16 points δ, δ_i, δ_ij are pairwise different. (5) δ not in E_ij for any i ≠ j.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{sfgFhRh}
Let $\Bbb P^2\subset\Bbb P^3$ be the plane passing  through $p_1,\ldots,p_5$.
Let 
$$\bold{dP}_4=\operatorname{Bl}_{p_1,\ldots,p_5}\Bbb P^2,$$ 
be the del Pezzo surface of degree $4$.
Recall that  $\overline{M}_{0,5}$ is the del Pezzo surface of degree $5$.
The~scattering amplitude map can be extended to 
a commutative diagram 
$$\begin{CD}
\operatorname{Sym}^2C      @>\overline{\bold\Lambda}>>  \bold{dP}_4\\
@V{a}VV         @VV{\Xi}V \\
\operatorname{Pic}^3C   @>{\bold\Lambda}>>  \overline{M}_{0,5}
\end{CD}$$
where horizontal arrows are rational maps.
The maps can be described as follows:
\begin{enumerate}
\item For any $(x,y)\in\operatorname{Sym}^2C$, consider the secant line $\ell_{xy}\subset\Bbb P^3$ 
connecting $x$ and $y$ (or the tangent line to $C$ at $x$  if $x=y$). Then $\overline{\bold\Lambda}(x,y)=\ell_{xy}\cap \Bbb P^2$.
\item  The map $\Xi$ is given by projecting $p_1,\ldots,p_5$ from a varying point of $\Bbb P^2$ (cf.~\cite{CT_Cont}).
It  blows down
 the conic passing through $p_1,\ldots,p_5$ to the point  
$$o:=\left(\varphi_K(p_1),\ldots,\varphi_K(p_5)\right)\in M_{0,5}.$$
\item $a(x,y)=\Cal O(P-x-y)$. The map $a$ gives $\operatorname{Sym}^2C$ as the blow-up of $\operatorname{Pic}^3C$ at $\delta$.
\end{enumerate}

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{Lemma}\label{SFgSFhF} We have divisors $\Cal D,\Cal D_i,\Cal D_{ij}\subset C^5\setminus\bigcup\limits_{i<j}\Delta_{ij}$ that parametrize configurations of marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_5\in C$ satisfying any of the following equivalent conditions: \begin{enumerate} \item $\delta\in E$ or $\delta_i\in E_i$ or $\delta_{ij}\in E_{ij}$, respectively. \item $\delta\in R$ or $\delta_i\in R$ or $\delta_{ij}\in R$, respectively. \item $\delta-K$ or $P-K-2p_i$ or $P-2p_i-2p_j$, respectively, is an effective divisor. \end{enumerate} Divisors $\Cal D,\Cal D_i,\Cal D_{ij}$ can also be characterized using geometric conditions: \begin{enumerate} \item[($\Cal D$)] the unique quadric surface in $\Bbb P^3$ containing $C$ is singular. \item[($\Cal D_i$)] the tangent line at $p_i$ to $C\subset \Bbb P^3$ intersects $C$ at another point. \item[($\Cal D_{ij})$] tangent lines at $p_i$ and $p_j$ intersect. \end{enumerate} \end{Lemma}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{vasdgsgG}
Let $X$ be the blow-up of $\operatorname{Pic}^3C$ in $16$ points $\delta$, $\delta_i$ and $\delta_{ij}$
with exceptional divisors $\Delta$, $\Delta_i$, $\Delta_{ij}$.
Let $\Bbb E$, $\Bbb E_i$, $\Bbb E_{ij}$ be the proper transforms of divisors $E$, $E_i$, $E_{ij}$.

The scattering amplitude rational map ${\bold\Lambda}$ induces a finite $4:1$ morphism $X\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{\overline{\bold\Lambda}}\bold{dP}_4$.
The~preimages of sixteen $(-1)$-curves of $\bold{dP}_4$ are the following pairs:
$$\overline{\bold\Lambda}^{-1}(\hbox{\rm conic through $p_1,\ldots,p_5$})=\Delta\cup\Bbb E;$$
$$\overline{\bold\Lambda}^{-1}(\hbox{\rm exceptional divisor over $p_i$})=\Delta_{i}\cup\Bbb E_{i};$$
$$\overline{\bold\Lambda}^{-1}(\hbox{\rm line through $p_i$, $p_j$})=\Delta_{ij}\cup\Bbb E_{ij}.$$
The restriction of $\overline{\bold\Lambda}$ to each  ``$\Delta\cup E$'' pair depends on on whether
$(p_1,\ldots,p_5)$ is contained in the corresponding configuration divisor ``$\Cal D$''  in $C^5$:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(yes)]       $\overline{\bold\Lambda}$ is ramified (of order $2$) along $``\Delta''$ and has degree $2$ along ``$\Bbb E$''.
\item[(no)] $\overline{\bold\Lambda}$ is an isomorphism along ``$\Delta$'' and has degree $3$ along ``$\Bbb E$''.
\end{enumerate}

theorem sDGSG {C : Type*} [AddCommGroup C] [Module ℝ C] [TopologicalSpace C] [T2Space C]
  (p1 p2 p3 p4 p5 p6 : C) (K : C) (delta deltaᵢ deltaᵢⱼ : C) (tau : C) (X : C) (Kum : C) (K3 : C) (Λ : C)
  (h1 : (p1, p2, p3, p4, p5) ∈ Cal D ∩ ⋂ i, Cal Dᵢ ∩ ⋂ i j, Cal Dᵢⱼ)
  (h2 : p1 ∈ WeierstrassPoints C ∧ p2 ∈ WeierstrassPoints C ∧ p3 ∈ WeierstrassPoints C ∧
        p4 ∈ WeierstrassPoints C ∧ p5 ∈ WeierstrassPoints C)
  (h3 : tau = involutionOfHyperellipticInvolution (Pic 3 C))
  (h4 : Kum = Pic 3 C / tau)
  (h5 : K3 = minimalResolution Kum)
  (h6 : X = BlowUp (Pic 3 C) {delta, deltaᵢ, deltaᵢⱼ})
  (h7 : K3 = X / tau) :
  ∃ (f : X → K3) (g : K3 → dP4),
    (Function.Surjective f ∧ Function.Surjective g) ∧
    (∀ x, f x ∈ deltaTypeDivisors C ∨ f x ∈ ETypeDivisors C) ∧
    (∀ x, g (f x) ∈ RationalCurves (K3)) ∧
    (∃ R : Set C, R ∈ HumbertCurve C ∧ R.card = 5)

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{sDGSG}
We have $(p_1,\ldots,p_5)\in\Cal D\cap\bigcap_i\Cal D_i\cap\bigcap_{ij}\Cal D_{ij}$ if and only if 
$p_1,\ldots,p_5$ are Weierstrass points of~$C$, i.e.~ramification points of $\varphi_K$.
Suppose that this is the case.
\begin{enumerate}
\item Let $p_6$ be the remaining Weierstrass point.
The $16$ special points are
$$\delta=K+p_6, \quad \delta_i=K+p_i,\quad \delta_{ij}=p_i+p_j+p_6.$$
Equivalently, this is the set of $2$-torsion points in $\operatorname{Pic}^0C$ shifted by $\delta$.
\item
Let $\tau$ be the involution of $\operatorname{Pic}^3C$ induced by the hyperelliptic involution of $C$.
Let~$\operatorname{Kum}=\operatorname{Pic}^3/\tau$ be the Kummer surface with $16$ double points and let $\operatorname{K3}$ be its minimal resolution.
Then $\tau$ lifts to $X=\operatorname{Bl}_{\delta,\delta_i,\delta_{ij}}\operatorname{Pic}^3$ and $\operatorname{K3}=X/\tau$. 
\item
The scattering amplitude $4:1$ cover $\overline{\bold\Lambda}:\,X\to\bold{dP}_4$ factors as
$$X\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\operatorname{K3}\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\bold{dP}_4.$$
\item The images of ``$\Delta$'' and ``$\Bbb E$'' type divisors give two configuration of $16$ rational curves in $\operatorname{K3}$.
Their images in $\operatorname{Kum}$ are the $16$~nodes and $16$ conics called ``tropes''.
The image of $R$ is a genus $5$ ``Humbert'' curve. 
\end{enumerate}

Suppose that this is the case.
\begin{enumerate}
\item Let $p_6$ be the remaining Weierstrass point.
The $16$ special points are
$$\delta=K+p_6, \quad \delta_i=K+p_i,\quad \delta_{ij}=p_i+p_j+p_6.$$
Equivalently, this is the set of $2$-torsion points in $\operatorname{Pic}^0C$ shifted by $\delta$.
\item
Let $\tau$ be the involution of $\operatorname{Pic}^3C$ induced by the hyperelliptic involution of $C$.
Let~$\operatorname{Kum}=\operatorname{Pic}^3/\tau$ be the Kummer surface with $16$ double points and let $\operatorname{K3}$ be its minimal resolution.
Then $\tau$ lifts to $X=\operatorname{Bl}_{\delta,\delta_i,\delta_{ij}}\operatorname{Pic}^3$ and $\operatorname{K3}=X/\tau$. 
\item
The scattering amplitude $4:1$ cover $\overline{\bold\Lambda}:\,X\to\bold{dP}_4$ factors as
$$X\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\operatorname{K3}\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{2:1}\bold{dP}_4.$$
\item The images of ``$\Delta$'' and ``$\Bbb E$'' type divisors give two configuration of $16$ rational curves in $\operatorname{K3}$.
Their images in $\operatorname{Kum}$ are the $16$~nodes and $16$ conics called ``tropes''.
The image of $R$ is a genus $5$ ``Humbert'' curve. 
\end{enumerate}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
We refer to \cite{GH} for the basic theory of  real algebraic curves and  Jacobians.
Let $C$ be a smooth projective complex algebraic curve of genus $g$ . 
We view the set of complex points $C(\Bbb C)$ as a Riemann surface. 
The curve $C$ endowed with a real structure (equivalently, an anti-holomorphic involution) 
is called an {\em  M-curve}\footnote{``M'' stands for ``maximal''.} if the set of real points $C(\Bbb R)\subset C(\Bbb C)$ has $g+1$ (the maximal possible number)
connected components $C_1,\ldots, C_{g+1}$. 
These ovals 
separate $C(\Bbb C)$ into two connected subsets interchanged by the complex conjugation $p\mapsto\bar p$.
Recall from Theorem~\ref{efvwb} that all smooth curves with $n=g+3$ marked points are MHV curves.
In this section we will study the scattering amplitude  of M-curves.

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{Review} We refer to \cite{GH} for the basic theory of real algebraic curves and Jacobians. Let $C$ be a smooth projective complex algebraic curve of genus $g$ . We view the set of complex points $C(\Bbb C)$ as a Riemann surface. The curve $C$ endowed with a real structure (equivalently, an anti-holomorphic involution) is called an {\em M-curve}\footnote{``M'' stands for ``maximal''.} if the set of real points $C(\Bbb R)\subset C(\Bbb C)$ has $g+1$ (the maximal possible number) connected components $C_1,\ldots, C_{g+1}$. These ovals separate $C(\Bbb C)$ into two connected subsets interchanged by the complex conjugation $p\mapsto\bar p$. Recall from Theorem~\ref{efvwb} that all smooth curves with $n=g+3$ marked points are MHV curves. In this section we will study the scattering amplitude of M-curves. \end{Review}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{awrgawrg}
It is well-known \cite{GH} that the set of real points  
$$\operatorname{Pic}^{d}(\Bbb R)\subset \operatorname{Pic}^{d}(\Bbb C)$$ 
of every component of the Picard group of an M-curve~$C$
is a union of 
$2^g$ connected components denoted by $\operatorname{Pic}_I^{d}(\Bbb R)$ and indexed by subsets $I\subset \{1,\ldots,g+1\}$ such that $|I|\equiv d\mod 2$.
Namely, if a divisor $D=\bar D$ is preserved by the complex conjugation  then the following conditions are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}
\item $\Cal O(D)\in \operatorname{Pic}_I^{d}(\Bbb R)$;
\item the divisor $D\cap C_i$ has odd degree if and only if $i\in I$.
\end{enumerate}
Each component $\operatorname{Pic}_I^{d}(\Bbb R)\subset\operatorname{Pic}^{d}(\Bbb R)$ is a torsor of the real Lie group 
$$\operatorname{Pic}^0_\emptyset(\Bbb R)\simeq \operatorname{U}(1)^g\simeq(\Bbb R/\Bbb Z)^g.$$
In what follows we mostly focus on the MHV component $\operatorname{Pic}^dC$, so that $d=g+1$.

Since all components $\operatorname{Pic}_I^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$ of an MHV M-curve are Zariski dense in $\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}(\Bbb C)$, 
${\bold\Lambda}$ is defined and unramified generically along them.
Moreover, 
$${\bold\Lambda}(\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}(\Bbb R))\subset M^?_{0,n}(\Bbb R),$$
where $?=A$ or $B$ depending on the type of the curve $C$. 
Indeed, if $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$ then we can choose $\varphi_L\in\Bbb R(C)$ a real  
meromorphic function
and the marked points will be mapped to points of the Riemann sphere according of type $A$ or~$B$.

[\cite{Huisman}]\label{adfhadrhadrha}$\ $
\begin{enumerate}
\item Every $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}_H(\Bbb R)$ is non-special and globally generated. 
\item $\varphi_L$ is unramified along $C(\Bbb R)=C_1\cup\ldots\cup C_{g+1}$.
\item $\varphi_L|_{C_i}:\,C_i\to\Bbb P^1(\Bbb R)$ is a real-analytic isomorphism for any~$i=1,\ldots,g+1$.
\item Fix $z_{g+1}\in C_{g+1}$. The map $C_1\times\ldots\times C_g\to\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}_H(\Bbb R)$ that sends $z_1,\ldots,z_g$ to
$\Cal O(z_1+\ldots+z_g+z_{g+1})$ is 
a real-analytic isomorphism.
\end{enumerate}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
By Corollary~\ref{dfvwefv}, for smooth {\em complex} curves, the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C\to M_{0,n}$
 has large degree $2^g$. However, Theorems~\ref{dfvwevev} and \ref{DSvSDb} show that  
 for smooth {\em real} M-curves line bundles in every fiber ``localize''
 into different connected components $\operatorname{Pic}_I^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$ of $\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$.
In other words, ${\bold\Lambda}$
 is  determined by open immersions (in the domain of ${\bold\Lambda}$)
$${\bold\Lambda}_I:\,\operatorname{Pic}_I^{g+1}(\Bbb R)\dashrightarrow M^?_{0,n}(\Bbb R).$$
Each component $\operatorname{Pic}_I^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$ carries a uniform probability measure $|A_I|$ given 
by the real-valued volume form $A_I$ translation-invariant under the real $g$-dimensional
torus $\operatorname{Pic}^0_\emptyset(\Bbb R)$. Viewed on $M^?_{0,n}(\Bbb R)$, this gives a differential form $A_I$
and a probability measure $|A_I|$ that we call a {\em scattering amplitude probability measure}.
%We will study some properties of these maps and densities in the remainder of this section.
We will typically compactify $M^?_{0,n}(\Bbb R)$ in some way and 
extend $\Lambda$ to its domain of definition
(so that in particular it will be determined at least generically along $E$ and $E_{ij}$).
Since not every reasonable compactification of $M^?_{0,n}(\Bbb R)$ is 
an orientable manifold, one has to remember (from calculus) that in this case the integral of a
form is not well-defined, however one can always integrate probability measures.
The scattering  measure of the Huisman's component is especially well-behaved.

Let $C$ be a smooth MHV M-curve with ovals $C(\Bbb R)=C_1\cup\ldots\cup C_{g+1}$. Furthermore, we assume that 
$p_i\in C_i$ for $i=1,\ldots,g+1$ and that
$p_{g+2},p_{g+3}\in C_{g+1}$ (type A) or $p_{g+2}=\bar p_{g+3}$ (type B).
We compactify $M_{0,n}$ by $(\Bbb P^1)^g\simeq(\overline{M}_{0,4})^g$
using the product of forgetful maps $$\pi_{i,g+1,g+2,g+3}:\,M_{0,n}\to M_{0,4}$$
for $i=1,\ldots,g$. The scattering amplitude map induces a real-analytic isomorphism
$$\Bbb R^g/\Bbb Z^g\simeq\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}_H(\Bbb R)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{\bold\Lambda}(\Bbb R\Bbb P^1)^g$$
and gives a positive real-analytic scattering amplitude probability measure on $(\Bbb R\Bbb P^1)^g$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[genus $1$]\label{sdcwefvw}
Let $C$ be an elliptic M-curve with two ovals, $X$ and~$Y$. 
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\includegraphics[height=1.5in]{CompX.pdf}\qquad
\includegraphics[height=1.5in]{CompY.pdf}
\caption{\small Two types of double covers $C(\Bbb R)\to\Bbb P^1(\Bbb R)$ of an elliptic $M$-curve $C$. A double cover from the Huisman component is on the right.}\label{wfegb}
\end{figure}
We can use a point $x\in X$
to identify $C(\Bbb R)$ with $\operatorname{Pic}^2(\Bbb R)$  by tensoring with~$\Cal O(x)$. We  denote the corresponding ovals of $\operatorname{Pic}^2(\Bbb R)$ by 
$X^{(2)}$ and~$Y^{(2)}$.
It is clear that $X+X=X^{(2)}$,
where the left hand side denotes the locus of line bundles $\Cal O(x+x')$ for $x,x'\in X$.
It is also clear that $X+Y=Y^{(2)}$.
We claim that $Y+Y=X^{(2)}$. Indeed,  
we have a continuous map $C\to\operatorname{Pic}^2(\Bbb R)$ which sends $z$ to $\Cal O(z+\bar z)$.
By~continuity, the image of this map has to be equal to $X^{(2)}$. In the notation of \ref{awrgawrg},
$$X^{(2)}=\operatorname{Pic}^2_\emptyset(\Bbb R)\quad\hbox{\rm and}\quad Y^{(2)}=\operatorname{Pic}^2_{\{1,2\}}(\Bbb R)=\operatorname{Pic}^2_{H}(\Bbb R)$$
is the Huisman component of $\operatorname{Pic}^2(\Bbb R)$.
If $L\in X^{(2)}$ then the map
$$\varphi_L:\,C(\Bbb R)\to\Bbb P^1(\Bbb R)$$ 
represents $C(\Bbb R)$ in the  form $y^2=f_4(x)$,
where $f_4(x)$ is a real polynomial with four real roots  (the left  side of Figure~\ref{wfegb}).
By tradition, one of these roots is usually moved to infinity.
Here $\varphi_L$ is projection onto the $x$-axis.
Note that restriction of $\varphi_L$ to either  $X$ or $Y$ is neither surjective nor injective.
By contrast, if~$L\in Y^{(2)}$, the map
$\varphi_L$ represents the same curve in the form  $y^2=f_4(x)$,
where $f_4(x)$ is a real polynomial with two pairs of  complex conjugate roots (the right side of Figure~\ref{wfegb}).
In accordance with \ref{jhsbdchjs,bdv}, the restriction of $\varphi_L$ to both $X$ and $Y$  is a real analytic isomorphism
with inverses given by 
$$x\mapsto \left(x,\sqrt{f_4(x)}\right),\quad x\mapsto \left(x,-\sqrt{f_4(x)}\right).$$
Next we suppose that $C$ is an MHV elliptic M-curve of type A or B.
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{EllTypeAB.pdf}
\caption{\small MHV elliptic M curves of Type A (left) and type B (right).}
\end{figure}
In other words, we add $4$ marked points so that, depending on the type,
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(A)] $p_1,p_2,p_3\in X$ and $p_4\in Y$
\item[(B)] $p_1\in X$, $p_2\in Y$ and $p_3=\bar p_4$ are complex-conjugate points.
\end{enumerate}
We have six special points $p_{ij}=\Cal O(p_i+p_j)\in\operatorname{Pic}^2 C$. Depending on the type,
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(A)] $p_{12},p_{13},p_{23}\in X^{(2)}$ and $p_{14},p_{24},p_{34}\in Y^{(2)}$.
\item[(B)] $p_{34}\in X^{(2)}$, $p_{12}\in Y^{(2)}$ and $p_{13}=\bar p_{14}$, $p_{23}=\bar p_{24}$
are complex-conjugate.
\end{enumerate}
The scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^2C\to\Bbb P^1$
is given by the line bundle
$$\Cal L=\Cal O(p_{12}+p_{34})\simeq\Cal O(p_{13}+p_{24})\simeq\Cal O(p_{14}+p_{23}).$$
In both cases (A) and (B), $\Cal L\in Y^{(4)}$, the Huisman component of $\operatorname{Pic}^2(\operatorname{Pic}^2 C)$.
The~restriction of ${\bold\Lambda}$ to both connected components $X^{(2)}$ and $Y^{(2)}$ of $\operatorname{Pic}^2(\Bbb R)$
is an analytic isomorphism with $\Bbb P^1(\Bbb R)$. In other words, the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}$ 
of an elliptic M-curve of type (A) or (B) looks like 
the projection onto the $x$-axis as on the right  side of Figure~\ref{wfegb}.
We get a $4$-parameter family of smooth scattering amplitude probability measures
given (up to normalization) 
by 
$$A={dx\over\sqrt{f_4(x)}},$$
where $f_4$ is a real polynomial with two pairs of  complex conjugate roots.
%Two real components of $\operatorname{Pic}^2(\Bbb R)$ give the same distribution.
A familiar $2$-parameter subfamily is given by
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ c c }
${M(a,b)\over\pi}{dx\over \sqrt{(x^2+a^2)(x^2+b^2)}}$
&\raisebox{-40pt}{{\includegraphics[height=1.2in]{ellipticK.pdf}}}\cr
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
where $a\ne b$ and $M(a,b)$ is the arithmetic-geometric mean of $a$ and $b$.

\item[(A)] $p_1,p_2,p_3\in X$ and $p_4\in Y$
\item[(B)] $p_1\in X$, $p_2\in Y$ and $p_3=\bar p_4$ are complex-conjugate points.
\end{enumerate}
We have six special points $p_{ij}=\Cal O(p_i+p_j)\in\operatorname{Pic}^2 C$. Depending on the type,
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(A)] $p_{12},p_{13},p_{23}\in X^{(2)}$ and $p_{14},p_{24},p_{34}\in Y^{(2)}$.
\item[(B)] $p_{34}\in X^{(2)}$, $p_{12}\in Y^{(2)}$ and $p_{13}=\bar p_{14}$, $p_{23}=\bar p_{24}$
are complex-conjugate.
\end{enumerate}
The scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^2C\to\Bbb P^1$
is given by the line bundle
$$\Cal L=\Cal O(p_{12}+p_{34})\simeq\Cal O(p_{13}+p_{24})\simeq\Cal O(p_{14}+p_{23}).$$
In both cases (A) and (B), $\Cal L\in Y^{(4)}$, the Huisman component of $\operatorname{Pic}^2(\operatorname{Pic}^2 C)$.
The~restriction of ${\bold\Lambda}$ to both connected components $X^{(2)}$ and $Y^{(2)}$ of $\operatorname{Pic}^2(\Bbb R)$
is an analytic isomorphism with $\Bbb P^1(\Bbb R)$. In other words, the scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}$ 
of an elliptic M-curve of type (A) or (B) looks like 
the projection onto the $x$-axis as on the right  side of Figure~\ref{wfegb}.
We get a $4$-parameter family of smooth scattering amplitude probability measures
given (up to normalization) 
by 
$$A={dx\over\sqrt{f_4(x)}},$$
where $f_4$ is a real polynomial with two pairs of  complex conjugate roots.
%Two real components of $\operatorname{Pic}^2(\Bbb R)$ give the same distribution.
A familiar $2$-parameter subfamily is given by
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ c c }
${M(a,b)\over\pi}{dx\over \sqrt{(x^2+a^2)(x^2+b^2)}}$
&\raisebox{-40pt}{{\includegraphics[height=1.2in]{ellipticK.pdf}}}\cr
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
where $a\ne b$ and $M(a,b)$ is the arithmetic-geometric mean of $a$ and $b$.

It is clear that $X+X=X^{(2)}$, where the left hand side denotes the locus of line bundles $\Cal O(x+x')$ for $x,x'\in X$. It is also clear that $X+Y=Y^{(2)}$. We claim that $Y+Y=X^{(2)}$. Note that restriction of $\varphi_L$ to either $X$ or $Y$ is neither surjective nor injective. By contrast, if~$L\in Y^{(2)}$, the map $\varphi_L$ represents the same curve in the form $y^2=f_4(x)$, where $f_4(x)$ is a real polynomial

It is clear that $X+X=X^{(2)}$, where the left hand side denotes the locus of line bundles $\Cal O(x+x')$ for $x,x'\in X$.

If $L\in X^{(2)}$ then the map $$\varphi_L:\,C(\Bbb R)\to\Bbb P^1(\Bbb R)$$ represents $C(\Bbb R)$ in the form $y^2=f_4(x)$, where $f_4(x)$ is a real polynomial with four real roots (the left side of Figure~\ref{wfegb}).

By contrast, if~$L\in Y^{(2)}$, the map $\varphi_L$ represents the same curve in the form $y^2=f_4(x)$, where $f_4(x)$ is a real polynomial with two pairs of complex conjugate roots (the right side of Figure~\ref{wfegb}).

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[genus $1$ -- continued]
In type (A), the real form of $M_{0,4}$ is $M^A_{0,4}(\Bbb R)=M_{0,4}(\Bbb R)$, the configuration space of $4$ points in $\Bbb P^1(\Bbb R)$.
Viewing $M_{0,4}(\Bbb C)$ as a Riemann sphere punctured at $0,1,\infty$ via the cross-ratio map,
$M_{0,4}(\Bbb R)$ is identified with the real axis without $0,1,\infty$ and $\overline{M}_{0,4}(\Bbb R)$
with $\Bbb P^1(\Bbb R)$, the equator of the Riemann sphere.
The~scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^2(\Bbb R)\to \overline{M}_{0,4}(\Bbb R)$ has the property that
\begin{equation}\label{sgshrHR}
{\bold\Lambda}(p_{14})={\bold\Lambda}(p_{23})=0,\quad {\bold\Lambda}(p_{13})={\bold\Lambda}(p_{24})=1,\quad {\bold\Lambda}(p_{12})={\bold\Lambda}(p_{34})=\infty.
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
In type (B), the real form is $M^B_{0,4}(\Bbb R)$, the configuration space of two real and two complex-conjugate points in $\Bbb P^1(\Bbb C)$.
If $\lambda=(p_1,p_2;p_3,p_4)$ is their cross-ratio then
$$\overline\lambda=(p_1,p_2;p_4,p_3)=1-\lambda.$$
Therefore,
$$M^{2,1}_{0,4}(\Bbb R)=\left\{\operatorname{Re}(z)={1\over2}\right\}\subset\Bbb C$$
and the compactification $\overline{M}^{2,1}_{0,4}(\Bbb R)$ is obtained by adding a single point (at $\infty$).
The~scattering amplitude map ${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Pic}^2(\Bbb R)\to \overline{M}^{2,1}_{0,4}(\Bbb R)$ still has property \eqref{sgshrHR}
but now only $\infty$ is part of the real locus $\overline{M}^{2,1}_{0,4}(\Bbb R)$.
This corresponds to the fact that points $p_{13}=\bar p_{14}, p_{23}=\bar p_{24}$ are not in $\operatorname{Pic}^2(\Bbb R)$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Real planar locus W]
Recall from \ref{evwevwev} that the planar locus 
$W\subset \operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$ has codimension $3$ and (for a general curve)
parametrizes  realizations of $C$ as a degree $g+1$ 
plane curve with ${g(g-3)\over 2}$ nodes away from the marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_n$.
Generically along $W$, the scattering amplitude map is resolved by the blow-up \eqref{agargasr} of $W$
with exceptional divisor $\hat W$ and
the scattering amplitude form vanishes
along $\hat W$ with multiplicity $2$. 
Suppose that $C$ is a generic real MHV M-curve. Then line bundles in $W(\Bbb R)$
give realizations of $C$ as {\em Harnack curves}, more precisely real plane curves of degree $g+1$ and genus $g$ with $g+1$ real components.
These curves have ${g(g-3)\over 2}$ acnodes, i.e.~isolated real points (where two complex-conjugate branches 
of the complex plane curve intersect transversally).
If $g$ is odd then all $g+1$ components are ovals (separate $\Bbb R\Bbb P^2$ into a disk and a M\"obius strip).
If $g$ is even, there are $g$ ovals and one pseudo-line (a generator of $\pi_1(\Bbb R\Bbb P^2)$), see~\cite{GH}.
It follows that a general real line in  $\Bbb R\Bbb P^2$ has even degree on all components of $C(\Bbb R)$ if $g$ is odd
and on all but one if $g$ is even. So  for a general real MHV M-curve
$$W(\Bbb R)\subset\operatorname{Pic}_\emptyset^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$$ if $g$ is odd. If $g$ is even,
$W(\Bbb R)$ is contained in the union of $\operatorname{Pic}_I^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$ for $|I|=1$. 
It~follows that the scattering amplitude probability measures 
$|A_\emptyset|$ ($g$ odd) and $|A_I|$ for $|I|=1$ ($g$ even) are the
only  ones 
that vanish to the order $2$ along $\hat W(\Bbb R)$.

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
[Real planar locus W]
Recall from \ref{evwevwev} that the planar locus 
$W\subset \operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$ has codimension $3$ and (for a general curve)
parametrizes  realizations of $C$ as a degree $g+1$ 
plane curve with ${g(g-3)\over 2}$ nodes away from the marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_n$.
Generically along $W$, the scattering amplitude map is resolved by the blow-up \eqref{agargasr} of $W$
with exceptional divisor $\hat W$ and
the scattering amplitude form vanishes
along $\hat W$ with multiplicity $2$. 
Suppose that $C$ is a generic real MHV M-curve. Then line bundles in $W(\Bbb R)$
give realizations of $C$ as {\em Harnack curves}, more precisely real plane curves of degree $g+1$ and genus $g$ with $g+1$ real components.
These curves have ${g(g-3)\over 2}$ acnodes, i.e.~isolated real points (where two complex-conjugate branches 
of the complex plane curve intersect transversally).
If $g$ is odd then all $g+1$ components are ovals (separate $\Bbb R\Bbb P^2$ into a disk and a M\"obius strip).
If $g$ is even, there are $g$ ovals and one pseudo-line (a generator of $\pi_1(\Bbb R\Bbb P^2)$), see~\cite{GH}.
It follows that a general real line in  $\Bbb R\Bbb P^2$ has even degree on all components of $C(\Bbb R)$ if $g$ is odd
and on all but one if $g$ is even. So  for a general real MHV M-curve
$$W(\Bbb R)\subset\operatorname{Pic}_\emptyset^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$$ if $g$ is odd. If $g$ is even,
$W(\Bbb R)$ is contained in the union of $\operatorname{Pic}_I^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$ for $|I|=1$. 
It~follows that the scattering amplitude probability measures 
$|A_\emptyset|$ ($g$ odd) and $|A_I|$ for $|I|=1$ ($g$ even) are the
only  ones 
that vanish to the order $2$ along $\hat W(\Bbb R)$.
context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{Review}[Real planar locus W] Recall from \ref{evwevwev} that the planar locus $W\subset \operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$ has codimension $3$ and (for a general curve) parametrizes realizations of $C$ as a degree $g+1$ plane curve with ${g(g-3)\over 2}$ nodes away from the marked points $p_1,\ldots,p_n$. Generically along $W$, the scattering amplitude map is resolved by the blow-up \eqref{agargasr} of $W$ with exceptional divisor $\hat W$ and the scattering amplitude form vanishes along $\hat W$ with multiplicity $2$. Suppose that $C$ is a generic real MHV M-curve. Then line bundles in $W(\Bbb R)$ give realizations of $C$ as {\em Harnack curves}, more precisely real plane curves of degree $g+1$ and genus $g$ with $g+1$ real components. These curves have ${g(g-3)\over 2}$ acnodes, i.e.~isolated real points (where two complex-conjugate branches of the complex plane curve intersect transversally). If $g$ is odd then all $g+1$ components are ovals (separate $\Bbb R\Bbb P^2$ into a disk and a M\"obius strip). If $g$ is even, there are $g$ ovals and one pseudo-line (a generator of $\pi_1(\Bbb R\Bbb P^2)$), see~\cite{GH}. It follows that a general real line in $\Bbb R\Bbb P^2$ has even degree on all components of $C(\Bbb R)$ if $g$ is odd and on all but one if $g$ is even. So for a general real MHV M-curve $$W(\Bbb R)\subset\operatorname{Pic}_\emptyset^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$$ if $g$ is odd. If $g$ is even, $W(\Bbb R)$ is contained in the union of $\operatorname{Pic}_I^{g+1}(\Bbb R)$ for $|I|=1$. It~follows that the scattering amplitude probability measures $|A_\emptyset|$ ($g$ odd) and $|A_I|$ for $|I|=1$ ($g$ even) are the only ones that vanish to the order $2$ along $\hat W(\Bbb R)$. \end{Review}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{qfvqev}
Let $C$ be a smooth MHV M-curve of genus $2$ given by the equation $y^2=f(x)$,
where $f$ is a real polynomial of degree $5$ with $5$ distinct real roots.
We denote the ovals of $C(\Bbb R)$ by $C_1$, $C_2$ and $C_3$. Like in Theorem~\ref{sGARSGARHA},
we assume that 
$$p_i\in C_i\quad  \hbox{\rm for}\quad i=1,2,3$$
and that
$$p_4,p_5\in C_3\quad\hbox{\rm (type A)\quad or}\quad p_4=\bar p_5\quad\hbox{\rm    (type B)}.$$
See Figure~\ref{wEGsg}. We~follow notation of \ref{sfgasgsRH} for special points and divisors. In particular, 
$$K\in\operatorname{Pic}_{\emptyset}^2(\Bbb R)\quad  \hbox{\rm and}\quad P=p_1+\ldots+p_5\in\operatorname{Pic}_{\{1,2,3\}}^5(\Bbb R).$$
The special points $\delta=P-K$,\quad  $\delta_{i}=K+p_i$ and $\delta_{ij}=P-p_i-p_j$ are distributed among four 
components of 
$\operatorname{Pic}^3(\Bbb R)$ as follows:
\medskip
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ |c||c|c|c|c|} 
 \hline
 Type  & $\operatorname{Pic}_H^3(\Bbb R)$ & $\operatorname{Pic}_{\{1\}}^3(\Bbb R)$ & $\operatorname{Pic}_{\{2\}}^3(\Bbb R)$ & $\operatorname{Pic}_{\{3\}}^3(\Bbb R)$\\ 
 \hline
 A & $\delta$, $\delta_{45}$, $\delta_{34}$, $\delta_{35}$  & $\delta_1$, $\delta_{23}$, $\delta_{24}$, $\delta_{25}$  & $\delta_2$, $\delta_{13}$, $\delta_{14}$, $\delta_{15}$  & $\delta_3$, $\delta_4$, $\delta_5$, $\delta_{12}$ \\ 
 B & $\delta$, $\delta_{45}$ & $\delta_1$, $\delta_{23}$ & $\delta_2$, $\delta_{13}$ & $\delta_3$, $\delta_{12}$\\
 \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\medskip
The map $\varphi_P$ embeds $C(\Bbb R)$ into $\Bbb P^3(\Bbb R)$.  
Let $\Bbb P^2\subset\Bbb P^3$ be the plane passing  through $p_1,\ldots,p_5$.
Let $\bold{dP}_4=\operatorname{Bl}_{p_1,\ldots,p_5}\Bbb P^2$ be the quartic  del Pezzo surface.
Depending on the type of the MHV M-curve $C$, there are two possibilities for its real form:
$$\bold{dP}_4^A(\Bbb R)=\operatorname{Bl}_{p_1,\ldots,p_5}\Bbb P^2(\Bbb R)\quad \hbox{\rm or}\quad \bold{dP}_4^B(\Bbb R)=\operatorname{Bl}_{p_1,p_2,p_3}\Bbb P^2(\Bbb R).$$

  (p1 p2 p3 p4 p5 : C)
  (h_p1 : p1 ∈ Set.univ) (h_p2 : p2 ∈ Set.univ) (h_p3 : p3 ∈ Set.univ) (h_p4 : p4 ∈ Set.univ) (h_p5 : p5 ∈ Set.univ)
  (h_p4_p5 : p4 = p5 ∨ p4 ≠ p5)
  (h_Bl_Pic3C : Bl_Pic3C = Bl (Pic3C) (Finset.univ : Finset C))

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
By Theorem~\ref{DSvSDb}, 
$\operatorname{Pic}^{3}(\Bbb R)$ is disjoint from the ramification divisor $R$.\footnote{
Since $\delta\in\operatorname{Pic}^{3}(\Bbb R)$, this has a curious geometric consequence:
the unique quadric surface in $\Bbb P^3$ containing $C$ is smooth (by Lemma~\ref{SFgSFhF}).}
This~implies by Theorem~\ref{vasdgsgG} that the
degree $4$ morphism
$$\overline{\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Bl}_{16}\operatorname{Pic}^3C(\Bbb R)\to\operatorname{Bl}_{p_1,\ldots,p_5}\Bbb P^2(\Bbb R)\quad \hbox{\rm in type (A)},$$
$$\overline{\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Bl}_8\operatorname{Pic}^3C(\Bbb R)\to\operatorname{Bl}_{p_1,p_2,p_3}\Bbb P^2(\Bbb R)\quad \hbox{\rm in type (B)}$$
induced by the scattering amplitude map 
is a real-analytic isomorphism on each connected component $\operatorname{Pic}^3_IC(\Bbb R)$
blown up at $4$ (in type A) or $2$ (in type B) points.
Note that topologically each of these components 
is a connected sum of a torus $T^2$
with $4$ (in type A) or $2$ (in type B) $\Bbb R\Bbb P^2$'s while
$\bold{dP}_4(\Bbb R)$ is a connected sum of $6$ (in type A) or $4$ (in type B) $\Bbb R\Bbb P^2$'s.
The scattering amplitude map $\overline{\bold\Lambda}$ provides a real-analytic isomorphism
between these (non-orientable) surfaces.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{asgasrghar}
By Theorem~\ref{vasdgsgG}, the preimage of each $(-1)$-curve in $\bold{dP}_4$
is the union of the ``$\Delta$'' and ``$E$''-type divisors, where the former is a $\Bbb P^1$
and the latter is a copy of~$C$.  %% , which contains three real ovals $C_1$, $C_2$, $C_3$.
In the following table we give the preimages under $\overline{\bold\Lambda}$ of real $(-1)$-curves in $\bold{dP}_4(\Bbb R)$.
Recall that the ``$E$''-type divisors are 
$$E=K+C,\quad E_i=P-p_i-C,\quad E_{ij}=C+p_i+p_j.$$
The range of indices $k, l$ in the table is $3,4,5$.
We use notation 
$$E(C_i)=K+C_i,\quad E_i(C_j)=P-p_i-C_j,\quad E_{ij}(C_k)=C_k+p_i+p_j.$$

\medskip
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ |c|c||c|c|c|c|} 
 \hline
  &  curve in $\bold{dP}_4(\Bbb R)$  & $\operatorname{Pic}_{H}^3(\Bbb R)$ & $\operatorname{Pic}_{\{1\}}^3(\Bbb R)$ & $\operatorname{Pic}_{\{2\}}^3(\Bbb R)$ & $\operatorname{Pic}_{\{3\}}^3(\Bbb R)$\\ 
 \hline

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
Back in our situation,
the scattering amplitude measure on $\bold{dP}_4(\Bbb R)$ is smooth and positive away from
four (in type A) or two (in type B)  projective lines that correspond to ``$\Delta$'' divisors in the Table~\ref{asgasrghar}.
Along these lines, the scattering amplitude form vanishes like in \eqref{sGsgav}.
These disjoint (-1)-curves can always be contracted to points giving morphisms $\bold{dP}_4(\Bbb R)\to (\Bbb R\Bbb P^1)^2$.
Combining everything together gives a commutative diagram
$$\begin{CD}
\operatorname{Bl} \operatorname{Pic}^3_I(\Bbb R) @>\overline{\bold\Lambda}>> \bold{dP}_4(\Bbb R)\\
@VVV                                                  @VVV \\
\operatorname{Pic}^3_I(\Bbb R) @>\simeq>>      (\Bbb R\Bbb P^1)^2              
\end{CD}$$
where vertical arrows are blow-downs of four (type A) or two (type B) projective lines and the bottom arrow is a real-analytic isomorphism
(note that algebraically these varieties can't be more different!).
It follows that we can view the scattering amplitude measure on $\bold{dP}_4(\Bbb R)$
as a smooth positive probability measure on $(\Bbb R\Bbb P^1)^2$ (subject to M\"obius transformations of coordinates  as in Remark~\ref{argewrgrg}).

theorem argerhqer
    {V : Type*} [AddCommGroup V] [Module ℝ V] [TopologicalSpace V] [T2Space V]
    (dP4 dP5 : Type*) [MetricSpace dP4] [MetricSpace dP5]
    (Pic3_H : Type*) [MetricSpace Pic3_H]
    (Bl_Pic3_H : Type*) [MetricSpace Bl_Pic3_H]
    (delta_34 delta_35 delta_45 : V)
    (Lambda : Bl_Pic3_H → dP5)
    (h_Lambda : Function.Bijective Lambda)
    (h_continuous : Continuous Lambda)
    (h_contract : ∃ (conic : Set dP4), IsClosed conic ∧
      ∃ (exceptional_divisor : Set dP4), IsClosed exceptional_divisor ∧
      conic = exceptional_divisor)
    (h_dP4_to_dP5 : dP4 → dP5)
    (h_dP5_exc_div : Set dP5)
    (h_exc_div_contracted : ∃ (lines : Finset (Set dP5)),
      lines.card = 10 ∧
      ∀ l ∈ lines, IsClosed l ∧
      ∃ (transform : Set dP4), IsClosed transform ∧
      (h_dP4_to_dP5 '' transform) = l)
    (h_cross_ratios : dP5 → (ℝ × ℝ))
    (h_cross_ratios_contract : ∀ x : dP5,
      ∃ (l1 l2 : Set dP5), l1 ∈ h_exc_div_contracted.choose ∧
      l2 ∈ h_exc_div_contracted.choose ∧
      h_cross_ratios x = (1345, 2345))
    (h_diagram : ∃ (comm_diagram : Bl_Pic3_H → dP5 → Pic3_H → (ℝ × ℝ)),
      comm_diagram = fun b p h => (h_cross_ratios p, h_cross_ratios p))
    : ∃ (isomorphism : Bl_Pic3_H ≃ dP5),
      Function.Bijective isomorphism ∧
      Continuous isomorphism ∧
      ∀ x : Bl_Pic3_H, h_cross_ratios (isomorphism x) = (1345, 2345)

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
So far we have treated the Huisman component and the other connected components of $\operatorname{Pic}^3(\Bbb R)$ 
on the equal footing, but there is an important difference.
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ l c r}
\raisebox{10pt}{\includegraphics[height=0.9in]{results1A.pdf}}\quad$\ $&\includegraphics[height=1.1in]{results1B.pdf}&\includegraphics[width=1.4in]{results1C.pdf}\cr
\raisebox{10pt}{\includegraphics[height=0.9in]{results2A.pdf}}\quad$\ $&\includegraphics[height=1.2in]{results2B.pdf}&\includegraphics[width=1.4in]{results2C.pdf}\cr
\raisebox{10pt}{\includegraphics[height=0.9in]{results3A.pdf}}\quad$\ $&\includegraphics[height=1.1in]{results3B.pdf}&\includegraphics[width=1.4in]{results3C.pdf}\cr
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\caption{Scattering amplitudes  in genus $2$, Huisman's component}\label{argerhqer}
\end{figure}
For simplicity, we consider Type A only.
Recall that the space $\bold{dP}_4$ is an artifact of the hyperelliptic case, the actual observable is not this surface but $\bold{dP}_5=\overline{M}_{0,5}$.
The map $\bold{dP}_4\to \bold{dP}_5$ is a contraction of the conic that corresponds to the exceptional divisor $\Delta$
only for the Huisman's component (see \ref{asgasrghar}), in which case
the scattering amplitude
map gives a real-analytic isomorphism
$${\bold\Lambda}:\,\operatorname{Bl}_{\delta_{45}, \delta_{34}, \delta_{35}}\operatorname{Pic}^3_H(\Bbb R)\to \bold{dP}_5=\overline{M}_{0,5}(\Bbb R).$$
The $10$ exceptional divisors of $\bold{dP}_5$ (the ``Petersen graph'') are images of the $10$ proper transforms $l_{ij}$ in $\bold{dP}_4$ of lines in $\Bbb P^2$ 
connecting points 
$p_i$ and $p_j$ of the conic pairwise. The lines $l_{34}$, $l_{35}$, $l_{45}$ are
images of the exceptional divisors $\Delta_{34}$, $\Delta_{35}$, $\Delta_{45}$ on $\operatorname{Bl}_{\delta_{45}, \delta_{34}, \delta_{35}}\operatorname{Pic}^3_H$.
These three lines on $\bold{dP}_5$ are contracted by the map 
$$\overline{M}_{0,5}\to(\Bbb P^1)^2=(\overline{M}_{0,4})^2$$ 
given by cross-ratios $1345$ and $2345$.
This gives a  diagram of Theorem~\ref{sGARSGARHA}
$$\begin{CD}
\operatorname{Bl}_{\delta_{45}, \delta_{34}, \delta_{35}}\operatorname{Pic}^3_H(\Bbb R) @>{\bold\Lambda}>\simeq> \overline{M}_{0,5}(\Bbb R)\\
@VVV                                                  @VV{1345\atop 2345}V \\
\operatorname{Pic}^3_H(\Bbb R) @>\simeq>>      (\overline{M}_{0,4}(\Bbb R))^2              
\end{CD}$$
where horizontal arrows are real-analytic isomorphisms.
To summarize, for the Huisman component we have a preferred choice of cross-ratios
that gives a smooth positive scattering amplitude probability measure on $ (\Bbb R\Bbb P^1)^2$.
For the reader's amusement, we include a few pretty examples of these probability density functions 
in Figure~\ref{argerhqer}, rendered using Algorithm~\ref{asrgarh}.

theorem qergarh (U : Type*) (rho : ℝ → ℝ → ℝ) (v : ℝ) (pi : Bl (0, 0) U → U)
    (hpi : pi = fun (u, v) => (u, u * v)) (hrho_cont : Continuous rho)
    (hrho_pos : ∀f (u, v) in nhds (0, 0), rho u v > 0)
    (hrho_lim : ∀ v, Filter.Tendsto (fun u => rho u v) (nhds 0) (nhds (rho 0 v))) :
    ∀ v, Filter.Tendsto (fun x => rho x (v * x) * |x|) (nhds 0) (nhds (rho 0 v))

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{qergarh}
For a different choice of two cross-ratios, the scattering amplitude measure of the Huisman's component, smooth on $\overline{M}_{0,5}(\Bbb R)$,
acquires a singularity in $(\Bbb R\Bbb P^1)^2$ of the same type as the scattering amplitude of a non-Huisman component
has already in the interior of $\overline{M}_{0,5}(\Bbb R)$. 
Consider the local picture as in Remark~\ref{argarsga},
the real blow-up $\pi:\,\operatorname{Bl}_{(0,0)}U\to U$, where $U$ is a real-analytic chart.
Then $\pi$ has the form $(u,v)\mapsto (u,uv)$.
Suppose the probability measure $\rho(u,v)|dudv|$ on the blow-up
is continuos and positive generically along the exceptional curve $u=0$,
with the limit function $\bar\rho(v)=\lim\limits_{u\to 0}\rho(u,v)$.
In coordinates $(x,y)$ of $U$, 
$$\lim_{x\to 0}{\rho(x,vx) |x|}=\bar\rho(v)$$
for every  slope $v$. So the restriction of $\rho(x,y)$ to every line $y=vx$ grows as $\bar\rho(v)\over |x|$
when $|x|\to 0$.
In Figure~\ref{adfadfbadf}, we show how the probability density function
of the smooth scattering amplitude from the top of Figure~\ref{argerhqer}
looks like in $(\Bbb R\Bbb P^1)^2$ for two different choices of cross-ratios.
In both pictures notice the line of zero probability density running through singularities.
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c}
\includegraphics[height=1in]{results4A.pdf}&\includegraphics[width=1in]{results4B.pdf}&
\includegraphics[height=1in]{results5A.pdf}&\includegraphics[width=1in]{results5B.pdf}\cr
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\caption{Rendering of singularities. Left:  $4123,5123$;\quad right: $1345,5123$.}\label{adfadfbadf}
\end{figure}

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
If $C$ is an MHV curve then $L$ has degree $0$ or $1$ on every component of $C$.
The on-shell diagram
is a trivalent graph with $d$ black circles (where $L$ has degree~$1$)
and the remaining white circles (where $L$ has degree~$0$).

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{Assumption}\label{zxfbzxfzf} By Lemma~\ref{SFbSFh} and Remark~\ref{AKHJDbfHSDB}, if $C$ is an MHV curve then every connected component of a subgraph of white circles of the on-shell diagram is a tree with at most one marked point. In addition, by Lemma~\ref{wefwetb}, a connected subcurve $A\subset C$ of arithmetic genus~$p$ should satisfy the following conditions: \begin{enumerate} \item If $p>0$ then $\deg L|_A\ge p+1$. \item If $\deg L|_A=p+1$ then $A$ contains at most $p+3$ marked points. \end{enumerate} From now on we will assume that all these conditions hold. \end{Assumption}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{wefvev4tbg2} Let $C$ be a maximally degenerate curve satisfying Assumption~\ref{zxfbzxfzf}. Then
its on-shell diagram has the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}
\item
Black circles 
are only connected to white megacircles and marked points.
\item
Each white megacircle is connected to exactly one marked point.
\item
The data of the on-shell diagram is equivalent to the data of triples in $\{1,\ldots,n\}$:
$$\Gamma=\{\Gamma_1,\ldots,\Gamma_d\}.$$
%A triple that corresponds to a black circle $B$ consists of marked points connected to $B$ and to white megacircles connected to $B$.
Each $\Gamma_i$ is associated to one of the $d$ black circles of the on-shell diagram. For such a circle, the associated $\Gamma_i$ 
consists of the markings that are either attached directly at that black circle, or sit on a white megacircle connected to it.
\end{enumerate}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[\cite{CT_Crelle}]\label{hjgcjghcj}
A maximally degenerate stable curve $C$  is an MHV curve
if and only if it is give by triples $\Gamma$ 
as in Lemma~\ref{wefvev4tbg2} that form a CT hypertree\footnote{See \cite{CT_Crelle} for a motivation %for  this terminology
and a more general definition for subsets other than triples.
There are  other notions of hypertrees in literature, so to distinguish our case we  use terminology  ``CT hypertree".}, 
i.e.~satisfy~\eqref{CondS}:
$$\bigl|\bigcup_{j\in S}\Gamma_j\bigr|\ge |S|+2
\quad\hbox{\rm for every $S\subset\{1,\ldots,d\}$}.$$
The scattering amplitude map is birational with the inverse map (called $v$ in \cite{CT_Crelle})
\begin{equation}\label{nbcxjhfx}
{\bold\Lambda}^{-1}:\,M_{0,n}\to\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C,
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
$${\bold\Lambda}^{-1}(q_1,\ldots,q_n)=v^*\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}(1),$$
where $v:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$
is a unique morphism such that $v(p_i)=q_i$ and $v^*\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}(1)\in \operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C$.

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
[\cite{CT_Crelle}]\label{hjgcjghcj}
A maximally degenerate stable curve $C$  is an MHV curve
if and only if it is give by triples $\Gamma$ 
as in Lemma~\ref{wefvev4tbg2} that form a CT hypertree\footnote{See \cite{CT_Crelle} for a motivation %for  this terminology
and a more general definition for subsets other than triples.
There are  other notions of hypertrees in literature, so to distinguish our case we  use terminology  ``CT hypertree".}, 
i.e.~satisfy~\eqref{CondS}:
$$\bigl|\bigcup_{j\in S}\Gamma_j\bigr|\ge |S|+2
\quad\hbox{\rm for every $S\subset\{1,\ldots,d\}$}.$$
The scattering amplitude map is birational with the inverse map (called $v$ in \cite{CT_Crelle})
\begin{equation}\label{nbcxjhfx}
{\bold\Lambda}^{-1}:\,M_{0,n}\to\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C,
\tag{\arabic{section}.\arabic{Theorem}.\arabic{et}}\addtocounter{et}{1}\end{equation}
$${\bold\Lambda}^{-1}(q_1,\ldots,q_n)=v^*\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}(1),$$
where $v:\,C\to\Bbb P^1$
is a unique morphism such that $v(p_i)=q_i$ and $v^*\Cal O_{\Bbb P^1}(1)\in \operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
The main goal of \cite{CT_Crelle} was 
to construct  {\em hypertree divisors} $D_\Gamma\subset M_{0,n}$ with  good properties.
Suppose that $\Gamma$ is an irreducible CT hypertree and $g\ge3$.
Then ${\bold\Lambda}^{-1}$ contracts a unique divisor $D_\Gamma\subset M_{0,n}$ 
%\item The  inverse scattering amplitude morphism ${\bold\Lambda}^{-1}$ \eqref{nbcxjhfx}
%induces a contracting\footnote{That is, ${\bold\Lambda}^{-1}$ does not extract divisors.}
%birational map ${\bold\Lambda}^{-1}:\, \overline{M}_{0,n}\dashrightarrow X_\Gamma$ to
 %the compactified Jacobian $X_\Gamma$ of $\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C$.
%\item  The hypertree 
given~by
\begin{itemize}
\item choosing a configuration of different points $p_1,\ldots,p_n\in\Bbb P^2$ such that different points $p_i,p_j,p_k$
are collinear if and only if $\{i,j,k\}\in\Gamma$,
\item projecting points $p_1,\ldots,p_n$ from a point 
$p\in\Bbb P^2$ to points $q_1,\ldots,q_n\in\Bbb P^1$,
\item and representing 
the datum $(\Bbb P^1;q_1,\ldots,q_n)$ by a point of $M_{0,n}$.
\end{itemize}
%\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{project.pdf}}
%\caption{Hypertree divisor 
%as the locus of projections}
%\label{KVpicture}
%\end{figure}
%\end{enumerate}
The reader will  notice a parallel with \ref{evwevwev}: the image $W_\Gamma:={\bold\Lambda}^{-1}(D_\Gamma)\subset\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C$
is  the analogue of the planar locus $W\subset\operatorname{Pic}^{g+1}C$ of a smooth MHV curve $C$ that parametrizes its presentations
as a plane curve of degree $g+1$. Like in the smooth case, the scattering amplitude form on $\operatorname{Pic}^{\vec d}C$
(when pulled back to $M_{0,n}$) vanishes along $D_\Gamma$ with multiplicity $2$ because
$W_\Gamma$ has codimension~$3$. 
This was noticed in  \cite{CT_Crelle} and \cite{MHV},
which both contain (the same) determinantal equation for $D_\Gamma$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{sVSGsrg}
Let $\Gamma$ be a CT hypertree and let $C$ be the corresponding maximally degenerate stable MHV curve.
Then $\Gamma$ is spherical if and only if
$C$ does not have a $2$-channel factorization (see Definition~\ref{FbfhFHF}) and
admits a real algebraic  structure such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item $C$ is a stable limit of smooth pointed M-curves $C_t$ of type A (see Definition~\ref{SfbXfbSDb}).
Let ~$C(\Bbb R)=W_1\cup\ldots\cup W_{g+1}$ be the union of images of $g+1$ ovals of $C_t(\Bbb R)$.
\item No $W_i$ is an acnode, i.e.~a real node which is a  connected component of $C(\Bbb R)$.
\item No $W_i$ is  contained in a degree $0$ component of $C$ (white circle of the on-shell diagram). Equivalently,
$W_i$ is not contracted to a point by the morphism $C\to\Sigma$.
\end{enumerate}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
The first non-MHV case is the following ``666 Puzzle''. Let $C$ be a genus $4$ curve with $6$ marked points $p_1,\ldots p_6$. 
A choice of a degree $6$ line bundle $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^6C$ realizes $C$ as a $6$-nodal sextic in $\Bbb P^2$ with $6$ marked points. 
This gives a generically finite rational map $\Lambda$ from $\operatorname{Pic}^6C$ to $X(3,6)$, which has dimension~$4$. 
What is the degree of $\Lambda$? An easier case for $k=3$ would be to consider nodal genus~$2$ quartics in $\Bbb P^2$ with $5$ marked points but by the Gale duality this case is equivalent to the MHV case $g=k=2$, so the degree of $\Lambda$ will be $4$.

A choice of a degree $6$ line bundle $L\in\operatorname{Pic}^6C$ realizes $C$ as a $6$-nodal sextic in $\Bbb P^2$ with $6$ marked points. This gives a generically finite rational map $\Lambda$ from $\operatorname{Pic}^6C$ to $X(3,6)$, which has dimension~$4$. What is the degree of $\Lambda$?
